days.title: L'imperfetto, la forma
days.objective: Form the imperfect tense of regular verbs and of essere, fare, bere and dire.
days.paretoFocus: The imperfect is the second past tense of Italian, and without it you cannot describe anything that used to be true.
drills: Write the six imperfect forms of parlare, leggere and dormire from memory, then check the endings.
Say five sentences about what you did every day when you were ten years old.
Take five verbs you already know and turn each present-tense sentence into the imperfect.

items: What are the three sets of imperfect endings, one per conjugation?
Which four common verbs have an irregular imperfect stem?
How do you say 'we used to live in Naples'?

activities.title: L'imperfetto: come si forma
activities.intro: The imperfect is built on the verb stem plus one set of endings: -vo, -vi, -va, -vamo, -vate, -vano, with the vowel of the infinitive kept in front. So parlare gives parlavo, leggere gives leggevo, dormire gives dormivo. Only essere is truly irregular, while fare, bere and dire use their older long stems.

items.hr: Da bambino parlavo sempre con mia nonna.
items.en: As a child I always talked with my grandmother.
items.note: parlare -> parlavo, parlavi, parlava, parlavamo, parlavate, parlavano

items.hr: Ogni sera leggevamo un libro insieme.
items.en: Every evening we used to read a book together.
items.note: leggere -> leggevo, the -e- of the infinitive stays

items.hr: Marta dormiva fino a tardi.
items.en: Marta used to sleep until late.
items.note: dormire -> dormivo, the -i- of the infinitive stays

items.hr: Quando ero piccola, avevo i capelli lunghi.
items.en: When I was little, I had long hair.
items.note: essere -> ero, eri, era, eravamo, eravate, erano

items.hr: Mio padre faceva il pane e beveva un caffè.
items.en: My father used to make bread and drink a coffee.
items.note: fare -> facevo, bere -> bevevo, dire -> dicevo

activities.title: Le forme dell'imperfetto
activities.intro: Practise producing imperfect forms, including the four irregular stems.

questions.prompt: Choose the correct imperfect form of 'bere': Da piccolo io ___ molto latte.
questions.explanation: Bere uses the old stem bev-, so the imperfect is bevevo, bevevi, beveva.

questions.prompt: Which sentence has the correct imperfect of essere?
questions.explanation: Essere is the only verb with a fully irregular imperfect: ero, eri, era, eravamo, eravate, erano.

questions.prompt: Complete with the imperfect of 'abitare': Prima di Roma, loro ___ a Napoli.
questions.explanation: Third person plural of an -are verb in the imperfect takes -avano.

questions.prompt: Complete with the imperfect of 'fare': Che lavoro ___ tuo nonno?
questions.explanation: Fare uses the stem fac-, giving facevo, facevi, faceva.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'Every summer we went to the sea.'
questions.explanation: A repeated action in the past takes the imperfect, and andare is regular here: andavamo.

activities.title: Com'era la tua vita da bambino?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A colleague asks about your childhood, so answer using imperfect forms.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Dove abitavi quando eri bambino?
lines.en: Where did you live when you were a child?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Abitavo in un paese piccolo vicino al mare.
lines.en: I lived in a small village near the sea.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: E che cosa facevi il pomeriggio?
lines.en: And what did you do in the afternoon?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Giocavo in strada con i miei amici fino alle otto.
lines.en: I played in the street with my friends until eight.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: I tuoi genitori lavoravano tutti e due?
lines.en: Did both your parents work?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sì, mia madre era maestra e mio padre faceva il meccanico.
lines.en: Yes, my mother was a teacher and my father was a mechanic.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Eravate una famiglia grande?
lines.en: Were you a big family?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sì, eravamo in sei e la casa non era mai silenziosa.
lines.en: Yes, there were six of us and the house was never quiet.

days.title: L'imperfetto per descrivere
days.objective: Use the imperfect to describe people, places, weather, age and habits in the past.
days.paretoFocus: Almost every description you will ever give of the past, from the weather to how someone looked, uses this one tense.
drills: Describe your childhood bedroom in four sentences, all in the imperfect.
Describe yesterday's weather without saying what you did.
Describe a relative you knew as a child: age, appearance and one habit.

items: Which tense do you use to say how old you were?
How do you say 'it was raining and it was cold'?
What does 'da studente' mean at the start of a sentence?

activities.title: Descrivere con l'imperfetto
activities.intro: The imperfect paints the picture: how things looked, how you felt, how old you were, what the weather was doing and what you regularly used to do. It has no clear beginning or end, so it never reports a single finished event. Two ongoing actions are joined with mentre.

items.hr: Quando avevo dieci anni, abitavo in una casa piccola.
items.en: When I was ten, I lived in a small house.
items.note: age in the past always uses avere in the imperfect

items.hr: Era una giornata fredda e pioveva.
items.en: It was a cold day and it was raining.
items.note: weather in the background is imperfect

items.hr: Mia zia era alta e portava sempre gli occhiali.
items.en: My aunt was tall and always wore glasses.
items.note: appearance plus a habit, both imperfect

items.hr: Da studente andavo a scuola in bicicletta ogni mattina.
items.en: As a student I went to school by bicycle every morning.
items.note: da + noun means 'when I was a ...'

items.hr: Mentre lavoravo, ascoltavo la radio.
items.en: While I was working, I listened to the radio.
items.note: mentre joins two ongoing actions, both imperfect

activities.title: Descrizioni al passato
activities.intro: Practise describing scenery, appearance and what used to happen regularly.

questions.prompt: Which sentence describes the weather as a background scene?
options: Faceva freddo e c'era il sole.
Ha fatto freddo e c'è stato il sole.
Fa freddo e c'era il sole.
Faceva freddo e c'è il sole.
questions.answer: Faceva freddo e c'era il sole.
questions.explanation: Background weather has no start or end point, so both verbs are in the imperfect.

questions.prompt: Choose the form that describes how Anna looked: Da bambina Anna ___ i capelli ricci.
questions.explanation: A lasting physical description in the past takes the imperfect of avere.

questions.prompt: Complete with the imperfect of 'esserci': In piazza ___ molta gente.
questions.explanation: Esserci in the imperfect gives c'era for a singular noun and c'erano for a plural one.

questions.prompt: Complete with the imperfect of 'portare': Il nonno ___ sempre un cappello grigio.
questions.explanation: Sempre signals a repeated habit, which the imperfect expresses.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'It was raining and I did not have an umbrella.'
questions.explanation: Both the weather and the state of not having something are background, so both verbs are imperfect.

activities.title: La casa dei nonni
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A friend asks you to describe your grandparents' house, so keep every verb in the imperfect.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Com'era la casa dei tuoi nonni?
lines.en: What was your grandparents' house like?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Era vecchia ma molto luminosa, e c'era un giardino dietro.
lines.en: It was old but very bright, and there was a garden behind it.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Che cosa facevate insieme?
lines.en: What did you do together?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: La domenica mangiavamo tutti in cucina e mia nonna cucinava per dieci persone.
lines.en: On Sundays we all ate in the kitchen and my grandmother cooked for ten people.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: E d'inverno? Faceva freddo?
lines.en: And in winter? Was it cold?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sì, faceva freddo e stavamo tutti vicino al camino.
lines.en: Yes, it was cold and we all stayed near the fireplace.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ti piaceva stare là?
lines.en: Did you like being there?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Mi piaceva moltissimo, perché non avevo mai fretta.
lines.en: I liked it a lot, because I was never in a hurry.

days.title: Imperfetto o passato prossimo
days.objective: Choose correctly between the imperfect and the passato prossimo inside the same sentence.
days.paretoFocus: This single choice separates learners who sound Italian from learners who sound translated, and it appears in every past-tense sentence you say.
drills: Write three sentences with mentre plus imperfect, each interrupted by one passato prossimo event.
Take a short story you know and mark which verbs are scenery and which are events.
Explain out loud in English why 'era stanco' and 'si è stancato' are different.

items: Which tense moves the story forward, and which one paints the scene?
Why is 'non sono uscito perché avevo mal di testa' correct?
Give one sentence containing both past tenses.

activities.title: Imperfetto o passato prossimo?
activities.intro: Use the passato prossimo for a completed single event that moves the story forward, and the imperfect for description, background, habit and states that were still going on. A useful test: if you could add 'and then what happened' after the verb, it is a passato prossimo. Reasons, feelings and settings stay in the imperfect.

items.hr: Mentre leggevo, è suonato il telefono.
items.en: While I was reading, the phone rang.
items.note: background (leggevo) interrupted by an event (è suonato)

items.hr: Ieri sera ho visto un film bellissimo.
items.en: Last night I saw a wonderful film.
items.note: one finished event with a clear end

items.hr: Il film era lungo, ma mi è piaciuto molto.
items.en: The film was long, but I liked it a lot.
items.note: era describes, è piaciuto reports

items.hr: Da bambini giocavamo in cortile, poi un giorno abbiamo cambiato città.
items.en: As children we played in the courtyard, then one day we moved city.
items.note: habit in the imperfect, the single event that ends it in the passato prossimo

items.hr: Non sono uscito perché avevo mal di testa.
items.en: I did not go out because I had a headache.
items.note: the action is passato prossimo, the reason is a state, so imperfect

activities.title: Scegliere il passato giusto
activities.intro: Decide which past tense each verb needs.

questions.prompt: Choose the correct form: Mentre ___ la strada, ho visto un vecchio amico.
questions.explanation: Mentre marks an action already in progress, which is the imperfect, and the event that cuts into it is the passato prossimo.

questions.prompt: Which sentence reports one single completed event?
options: Ieri ho comprato una macchina nuova.
questions.answer: Ieri ho comprato una macchina nuova.
questions.explanation: The other three describe a habit, a state or an appearance, which all take the imperfect.

questions.prompt: Complete with the past form of 'arrivare' that reports a single finished action: Mentre aspettavo alla fermata, l'autobus ___.
questions.explanation: Arrivare takes essere, and the arrival is the event that interrupts the waiting.

questions.prompt: Complete with the past form of 'dormire' that says what was already going on: Quando sei tornato a casa, io ___ già.
questions.explanation: The sleeping had no end point at that moment, so it is described with the imperfect.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'While we were eating, the light went out.'
questions.explanation: Andare takes essere, and the participle agrees with la luce, so it ends in -a.

activities.title: Che cosa è successo?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You explain a small incident, so mix background in the imperfect with events in the passato prossimo.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Perché sei arrivato così tardi?
lines.en: Why did you arrive so late?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Mentre venivo qui, ho perso l'autobus.
lines.en: While I was coming here, I missed the bus.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: E non hai preso un taxi?
lines.en: And you did not take a taxi?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: No, non avevo contanti, così ho aspettato quello dopo.
lines.en: No, I had no cash, so I waited for the next one.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Pioveva forte, vero?
lines.en: It was raining hard, wasn't it?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sì, pioveva e faceva anche freddo, poi il tempo è cambiato.
lines.en: Yes, it was raining and it was cold too, then the weather changed.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Almeno sei arrivato prima della riunione.
lines.en: At least you arrived before the meeting.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Per fortuna sì, perché la riunione cominciava alle dieci.
lines.en: Luckily yes, because the meeting was starting at ten.

days.title: Raccontare una storia al passato
days.objective: Tell a short past story in order using prima, poi, allora, così, improvvisamente and alla fine.
days.paretoFocus: Connectors are what turn a list of verbs into a story people actually want to listen to.
drills: Tell the story of your last weekend in six sentences, each opening with a different connector.
Take three unconnected past sentences and join them with poi, così and alla fine.
Retell a film you saw recently in five sentences of past narration.

items: Which connector introduces a consequence?
Which connector closes a story?
How do you say 'suddenly it started to rain'?

activities.title: Prima, poi, alla fine
activities.intro: A story needs signposts as well as tenses. Prima and poi order the events, allora and così give the consequence, improvvisamente introduces a surprise and alla fine closes the sequence. The events stay in the passato prossimo while the scenery around them stays in the imperfect.

items.hr: Sabato mi sono svegliato presto perché volevo andare al mercato.
items.en: On Saturday I woke up early because I wanted to go to the market.
items.note: event plus a reason that is a state

items.hr: Prima ho fatto colazione, poi sono uscito di casa.
items.en: First I had breakfast, then I left the house.
items.note: prima ... poi orders two events

items.hr: Faceva bel tempo, così sono andato a piedi.
items.en: The weather was nice, so I walked.
items.note: così introduces the consequence

items.hr: Improvvisamente ho incontrato Luca, che aspettava l'autobus.
items.en: Suddenly I met Luca, who was waiting for the bus.
items.note: the surprise is an event, what Luca was doing is background

items.hr: Alla fine abbiamo preso un caffè insieme e siamo tornati a casa.
items.en: In the end we had a coffee together and went home.
items.note: alla fine closes the story

activities.title: Mettere in ordine i fatti
activities.intro: Practise the connectors that hold a past narration together.

questions.prompt: Which word introduces the next step of a story?
questions.explanation: Poi means 'then' and moves the narration to the following event.

questions.prompt: Choose the pair that puts the events in order: ___ ho finito di lavorare, ___ sono andata in palestra.
questions.explanation: Prima marks the earlier event and poi the later one.

questions.prompt: Complete with the single word that means 'suddenly': ___ ha cominciato a piovere.
questions.explanation: Improvvisamente introduces an unexpected event and normally opens the sentence.

questions.prompt: Complete with the connector that means 'so, therefore': Non c'era posto a sedere, ___ siamo rimasti in piedi.
questions.explanation: Così links a situation to its consequence, and it carries an accent on the final i.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'Then we went out and we took the train.'
questions.explanation: Uscire takes essere with an agreeing participle, prendere takes avere with an invariable one.

activities.title: Il mio fine settimana
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. Tell your neighbour about your weekend, using a connector in every answer.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Allora, com'è andato il fine settimana?
lines.en: So, how did the weekend go?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Bene! Prima sono andata in centro, poi ho incontrato mia sorella.
lines.en: Well! First I went downtown, then I met my sister.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: E avete mangiato insieme?
lines.en: And did you eat together?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sì, il ristorante era pieno, così abbiamo preso un panino al parco.
lines.en: Yes, the restaurant was full, so we had a sandwich in the park.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Che bello. E il pomeriggio?
lines.en: How nice. And the afternoon?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Improvvisamente ha cominciato a piovere e siamo tornate a casa.
lines.en: Suddenly it started raining and we went back home.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Peccato per la pioggia!
lines.en: What a shame about the rain!

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Alla fine è stata una giornata tranquilla, e va benissimo così.
lines.en: In the end it was a quiet day, and that is perfectly fine.

days.title: I pronomi diretti
days.objective: Replace a direct object with mi, ti, lo, la, ci, vi, li or le and place it correctly.
days.paretoFocus: Without object pronouns you must repeat every noun, which instantly marks your Italian as beginner level.
drills: Answer five yes/no questions about objects around you without repeating the noun.
Write five sentences with lo, la, li and le, one of each.
Say three sentences with mi, ti and ci as objects.

items: Where does the direct object pronoun go with a conjugated verb?
Which pronoun replaces a feminine plural noun?
What happens to lo and la before a vowel?

activities.title: I pronomi diretti
activities.intro: A direct object pronoun replaces a noun that receives the action with no preposition. There are eight forms: mi and ti for one person, ci and vi for a group, and lo, la, li, le for things and other people, matched for gender and number. They go immediately before the conjugated verb, including before non. Lo and la become l' before a vowel, while li and le never shorten.

items.hr: Compri il giornale? Sì, lo compro ogni mattina.
items.en: Do you buy the newspaper? Yes, I buy it every morning.
items.note: lo replaces a masculine singular object

items.hr: Dove metti la valigia? La metto in camera.
items.en: Where do you put the suitcase? I put it in the bedroom.
items.note: la replaces a feminine singular object

items.hr: I biglietti? Li prendo io.
items.en: The tickets? I will get them.
items.note: li replaces a masculine plural object

items.hr: Le chiavi non le trovo.
items.en: I cannot find the keys.
items.note: le replaces a feminine plural object and still sits before the verb

items.hr: Ti aspetto davanti al bar alle otto.
items.en: I will wait for you in front of the bar at eight.
items.note: mi, ti, ci and vi mean me, you, us and you all

activities.title: Sostituire il complemento
activities.intro: Practise choosing the right direct object pronoun and putting it in the right place.

questions.prompt: Choose the pronoun: Hai la macchina? Sì, ___ uso tutti i giorni.
questions.explanation: Macchina is feminine singular, so it is replaced by la.

questions.prompt: Which sentence places the direct object pronoun correctly?
questions.explanation: The pronoun goes between non and the conjugated verb.

questions.prompt: Complete with the direct object pronoun for 'i quaderni': I quaderni? ___ metto nello zaino.
questions.explanation: A masculine plural object is replaced by li.

questions.prompt: Complete with the direct object pronoun that means 'us': Marco ___ invita spesso a cena.
questions.explanation: The first person plural object pronoun is the same word used for 'there' in other contexts.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'I know him well.'
questions.explanation: The pronoun comes first, then the verb, then the adverb.

activities.title: Al supermercato
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You are shopping with a friend, so answer every question with a direct object pronoun instead of repeating the noun.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Prendiamo il pane qui?
lines.en: Shall we get the bread here?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sì, lo prendiamo qui, è più fresco.
lines.en: Yes, we will get it here, it is fresher.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: E le mele? Ti servono?
lines.en: And the apples? Do you need them?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Le compro io, così le mangiamo domani.
lines.en: I will buy them, so we can eat them tomorrow.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Hai la lista della spesa?
lines.en: Do you have the shopping list?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ce l'ho, ma non la trovo nella borsa.
lines.en: I have it, but I cannot find it in my bag.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Va bene, ti aspetto alla cassa.
lines.en: All right, I will wait for you at the till.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Perfetto, ti raggiungo fra due minuti.
lines.en: Perfect, I will join you in two minutes.

days.title: I pronomi diretti con il passato prossimo
days.objective: Make the past participle agree with a direct object pronoun that comes before the verb.
days.paretoFocus: This agreement is invisible in speech for some forms but always marked in writing, and it is where most learners lose marks.
drills: Write five answers with lo, la, li and le plus a passato prossimo, checking each ending.
Say five sentences with l'ho and check whether the participle ends in -o or -a.
Take three sentences with a noun object and rewrite them with a pronoun.

items: What ending does the participle take after li?
Why is 'l'ho vista' correct when talking about Anna?
Does the participle change after mi and ti?

activities.title: L'accordo del participio
activities.intro: When a direct object pronoun comes before the passato prossimo, the past participle agrees with that pronoun in gender and number. Lo and la shorten to l' before ho, hai, ha and so on, and only the participle ending then tells you which one it was. Li gives -i and le gives -e.

items.hr: Hai visto Anna? Sì, l'ho vista ieri.
items.en: Have you seen Anna? Yes, I saw her yesterday.
items.note: la + ho = l'ho, and the participle ends in -a

items.hr: Ho comprato i biglietti: li ho comprati stamattina.
items.en: I bought the tickets: I bought them this morning.
items.note: li -> participio in -i

items.hr: Le foto? Le ho mandate a mia sorella.
items.en: The photos? I sent them to my sister.
items.note: le -> participio in -e

items.hr: Il libro l'ho finito ieri sera.
items.en: The book, I finished it last night.
items.note: lo + ho = l'ho, and the participle keeps the -o

items.hr: Non l'ho ancora letta, quella lettera.
items.en: I have not read it yet, that letter.
items.note: with l' the ending of the participle is the only clue to the gender

activities.title: L'accordo con il pronome
activities.intro: Practise the participle endings after a direct object pronoun.

questions.prompt: Choose the correct form: La pizza? Io ___ mangiata tutta.
questions.explanation: La becomes l' before ho, and the participle mangiata shows the object was feminine singular.

questions.prompt: Choose the correct agreement: Le chiavi? Le ho ___ sul tavolo.
questions.explanation: Le is feminine plural, so the participle takes -e.

questions.prompt: Write the past participle of 'vedere' with the right ending: I miei cugini? Li ho ___ domenica.
questions.explanation: Li is masculine plural, so the irregular participle visto becomes visti.

questions.prompt: Write the past participle of 'invitare' with the right ending: Ho telefonato a Sara e l'ho ___ a cena.
questions.explanation: The hidden pronoun in l'ho is la, so the participle is feminine singular.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'I bought them this morning.' (speaking about the shoes)
questions.explanation: Le scarpe is feminine plural, so the pronoun is le and the participle ends in -e.

activities.title: L'hai già visto?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A friend asks what you have already done, so answer with a pronoun plus an agreeing participle.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Hai visto la serie nuova?
lines.en: Have you seen the new series?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sì, l'ho vista tutta il fine settimana scorso.
lines.en: Yes, I watched all of it last weekend.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: E i libri che ti ho prestato?
lines.en: And the books I lent you?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Li ho letti e li ho messi nella tua borsa.
lines.en: I read them and I put them in your bag.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Grazie! Hai spedito le cartoline?
lines.en: Thanks! Did you send the postcards?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Le ho spedite ieri, così arrivano prima di Natale.
lines.en: I sent them yesterday, so they arrive before Christmas.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: E il regalo per Marta?
lines.en: And Marta's present?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: L'ho comprato online, ma non l'ho ancora incartato.
lines.en: I bought it online, but I have not wrapped it yet.

days.title: I pronomi indiretti
days.objective: Use mi, ti, gli, le, ci, vi to say to whom you speak, write, give or answer.
days.paretoFocus: The verbs of everyday contact, telephoning, writing, giving and answering, all need an indirect pronoun in Italian.
drills: List five verbs that are followed by a, then use each with an indirect pronoun.
Write three sentences with gli and three with le, keeping the genders clear.
Rewrite three sentences that name a person after the preposition a, using a pronoun instead.

items: Which pronoun means 'to him' and which one means 'to her'?
Does the past participle agree with an indirect pronoun?
How do you say 'I am writing to them' in everyday speech?

activities.title: I pronomi indiretti
activities.intro: An indirect object answers the question 'to whom'. Six forms cover it: mi and ti in the singular, ci and vi in the plural, plus gli for him and le for her, with gli again for the plural in normal spoken Italian. They sit before the verb like direct pronouns, but they never cause participle agreement.

items.hr: Telefono a Marco. Gli telefono ogni domenica.
items.en: I phone Marco. I phone him every Sunday.
items.note: gli means 'to him'

items.hr: Scrivo a mia madre. Le scrivo una lunga lettera.
items.en: I write to my mother. I write her a long letter.
items.note: le means 'to her'

items.hr: Che cosa mi consigli per il regalo?
items.en: What do you recommend to me for the present?
items.note: mi means 'to me'

items.hr: Ci hanno offerto un caffè.
items.en: They offered us a coffee.
items.note: the participle offerto stays in -o: no agreement with indirect pronouns

items.hr: Ai bambini gli leggo una storia ogni sera.
items.en: I read the children a story every evening.
items.note: in everyday Italian gli also covers 'to them'

activities.title: A chi?
activities.intro: Practise choosing between direct and indirect pronouns.

questions.prompt: Choose the pronoun: Hai risposto a Giulia? Sì, ___ ho risposto subito.
questions.explanation: Rispondere takes a, so the object is indirect, and the feminine singular indirect pronoun is le.

questions.prompt: Which verb is followed by an indirect object in Italian?
questions.explanation: Only telefonare needs the preposition a, so only it takes an indirect pronoun.

questions.prompt: Complete with the pronoun that means 'to him': Domani vedo Paolo e ___ do le chiavi.
questions.explanation: Dare is used with a, so the person receiving takes the masculine indirect pronoun.

questions.prompt: Complete with the pronoun that means 'to us': Il professore ___ spiega la regola.
questions.explanation: Spiegare a noi becomes an indirect pronoun placed before the verb.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'I am giving her a present.'
questions.explanation: Fare un regalo a qualcuno takes the indirect pronoun le for a woman.

activities.title: Un regalo per Sara
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You plan a present with a colleague, so use indirect pronouns for the people involved.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Che cosa regaliamo a Sara?
lines.en: What shall we give Sara?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Le regaliamo un libro, legge sempre in treno.
lines.en: Let's give her a book, she always reads on the train.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Buona idea. Hai parlato con Davide?
lines.en: Good idea. Have you spoken to Davide?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Gli ho scritto un messaggio, ma non mi ha ancora risposto.
lines.en: I wrote him a message, but he has not answered me yet.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ci puoi dire quanto costa?
lines.en: Can you tell us how much it costs?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Vi dico il prezzo domani, prima chiedo in libreria.
lines.en: I will tell you the price tomorrow, first I will ask in the bookshop.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: E agli altri colleghi che cosa diciamo?
lines.en: And what do we say to the other colleagues?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Gli mandiamo un messaggio stasera, così partecipano tutti.
lines.en: We will send them a message tonight, so everybody takes part.

days.title: I pronomi combinati
days.objective: Combine an indirect and a direct pronoun into forms like me lo, te la, glielo and ve le.
days.paretoFocus: Combined pronouns are everywhere in spoken Italian, in shops, at work and between friends, and they cannot be avoided once you know both series.
drills: Build the full grid: mi, ti, ci, vi each followed by lo, la, li, le.
Write four requests using me lo, te la, ce li and ve le.
Say three sentences with glielo, gliela and gliele about the same person.

items: What happens to the final i of mi, ti, ci and vi before another pronoun?
Why is glielo written as a single word?
How do you say 'I will lend it to you' to a friend?

activities.title: I pronomi combinati
activities.intro: When two pronouns meet, the indirect one comes first and its final i turns into e: mi lo becomes me lo, ti la becomes te la. Gli and le both become glie- and join the direct pronoun in one word: glielo, gliela, glieli, gliele. The pair still stands before the conjugated verb.

items.hr: Mi presti la macchina? Sì, te la presto volentieri.
items.en: Will you lend me the car? Yes, I will lend it to you gladly.
items.note: ti + la = te la

items.hr: Me lo puoi ripetere, per favore?
items.en: Can you repeat it to me, please?
items.note: mi + lo = me lo

items.hr: Il libro? Glielo do domani.
items.en: The book? I will give it to him tomorrow.
items.note: gli or le + lo = glielo, written as one word

items.hr: Ve le mando per posta.
items.en: I will send them to you by post.
items.note: vi + le = ve le

items.hr: Ce l'ha detto ieri sera.
items.en: He told it to us last night.
items.note: ci + lo = ce lo, and before ha it shortens to ce l'ha

activities.title: Due pronomi insieme
activities.intro: Practise building the fused two-pronoun forms and placing them correctly.

questions.prompt: Choose the correct pair: Mi dai il numero? Sì, ___ do subito.
questions.explanation: The indirect pronoun comes first and changes its vowel, and numero is masculine singular.

questions.prompt: Which is the correct single word for 'it (feminine) to her'?
questions.explanation: Both gli and le become glie- and are written joined to the direct pronoun.

questions.prompt: Complete with the two combined pronouns that mean 'it to me': ___ spieghi di nuovo, per favore?
questions.explanation: The indirect form changes its final vowel to e and stands in front of the masculine singular direct pronoun.

questions.prompt: Complete with the single combined word that means 'it to her': La ricetta? ___ scrivo su un foglio.
questions.explanation: Ricetta is feminine singular, so the combined form ends in -a.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'I am sending it to you tomorrow.'
questions.explanation: The indirect pronoun comes first, then the direct one, then the verb.

activities.title: Me lo presti?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You borrow and lend small things with a flatmate, so answer with two pronouns together.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Mi presti il caricabatterie?
lines.en: Will you lend me the charger?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Certo, te lo do subito.
lines.en: Of course, I will give it to you right away.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: E l'indirizzo di Anna? Ce l'hai?
lines.en: And Anna's address? Do you have it?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sì, te lo scrivo qui sul foglio.
lines.en: Yes, I will write it down for you here on the paper.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Le chiavi di casa le hai date a Luca?
lines.en: Did you give Luca the house keys?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Gliele ho date stamattina, prima di uscire.
lines.en: I gave them to him this morning, before going out.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Perfetto. Poi mi racconti tutto?
lines.en: Perfect. Will you tell me everything later?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Te lo racconto a cena, adesso ho fretta.
lines.en: I will tell you about it at dinner, right now I am in a hurry.

days.title: Il ne e il ci
days.objective: Use ne for quantities and di phrases, and ci for places and a phrases.
days.paretoFocus: These two little words appear in almost every shop conversation and in dozens of fixed expressions.
drills: Answer five quantity questions about your kitchen using ne plus a number.
Write three sentences with ci replacing a place you go to often.
Use ne with a past participle three times and check the ending.

items: Which particle replaces a phrase beginning with di?
Which particle replaces a place already mentioned?
What does the participle do after ne plus a number?

activities.title: Ne e ci
activities.intro: Ne replaces a quantity or a phrase with di, and is used even when English says nothing at all. Ci replaces a place or a phrase with a, especially with pensare and credere. Both stand before the verb, and after ne the past participle agrees with the quantity.

items.hr: Quante mele vuoi? Ne voglio tre.
items.en: How many apples do you want? I want three of them.
items.note: ne is obligatory with a quantity, even if English drops it

items.hr: Hai del pane? Sì, ne ho un po'.
items.en: Do you have any bread? Yes, I have a bit of it.
items.note: ne replaces di plus a noun

items.hr: Vai a Milano? Sì, ci vado domani.
items.en: Are you going to Milan? Yes, I am going there tomorrow.
items.note: ci replaces a place

items.hr: Pensi al lavoro? Sì, ci penso spesso.
items.en: Do you think about work? Yes, I think about it often.
items.note: pensare a plus a thing becomes ci penso

items.hr: Ne ho comprati due.
items.en: I bought two of them.
items.note: after ne the participle agrees with what is counted

activities.title: Quanti ne vuoi?
activities.intro: Practise choosing between ne and ci.

questions.prompt: Choose the particle: Quanti fratelli hai? ___ ho due.
questions.explanation: With a number the particle for 'of them' is obligatory in Italian.

questions.prompt: Choose the particle: Sei mai stato a Venezia? Sì, ___ sono stato l'anno scorso.
questions.explanation: A place already mentioned is replaced by the particle meaning 'there'.

questions.prompt: Complete with the particle that replaces 'di birra': Vuoi della birra? No, grazie, non ___ bevo.
questions.explanation: A phrase introduced by di is replaced by this particle placed before the verb.

questions.prompt: Complete with the particle that means 'there': In palestra ___ vado tre volte alla settimana.
questions.explanation: The place is already named at the start, so the particle repeats it before the verb.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'I bought three of them.' (speaking about the pizzas)
questions.explanation: Le pizze is feminine plural, so the participle agrees and ends in -e.

activities.title: Al mercato
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You are buying fruit at a market stall, so use ne and ci in your answers.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Buongiorno! Le arance sono molto buone oggi. Quante ne vuole?
lines.en: Good morning! The oranges are very good today. How many do you want?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ne prendo sei, grazie.
lines.en: I will take six, thank you.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: E del formaggio? Ne abbiamo di ottimo.
lines.en: And some cheese? We have some excellent one.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ne prendo un etto, ma non troppo stagionato.
lines.en: I will take a hundred grams, but not too mature.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Va bene. Viene spesso al mercato?
lines.en: All right. Do you come to the market often?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ci vengo ogni sabato mattina, prima delle nove.
lines.en: I come here every Saturday morning, before nine.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Bene, così trova sempre tutto fresco.
lines.en: Good, that way you always find everything fresh.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Esatto, ci penso sempre il venerdì sera.
lines.en: Exactly, I always think about it on Friday evening.

days.title: L'imperativo informale
days.objective: Give instructions, advice and orders to friends using the tu, noi and voi imperative.
days.paretoFocus: Directions, recipes, advice and everyday requests between friends all run on the informal imperative.
drills: Give five instructions to a friend for getting from your home to the station.
Turn three of those instructions into negative commands.
Write a three step recipe using the voi form.

items: What ending does an -are verb take in the tu imperative?
How do you form a negative command to one friend?
What happens when a pronoun follows di' or da'?

activities.title: L'imperativo con gli amici
activities.intro: For tu, an -are verb ends in -a and an -ere or -ire verb ends in -i. The noi form is the same as the present (andiamo) and the voi form is the present too (prendete). A negative command to one person is simply non plus the infinitive, and pronouns attach to the end of the informal forms.

items.hr: Parla più piano, per favore!
items.en: Speak more slowly, please!
items.note: parlare -> parla for tu

items.hr: Prendi l'autobus numero cinque e scendi in centro.
items.en: Take bus number five and get off in the centre.
items.note: -ere and -ire verbs end in -i for tu

items.hr: Non aprire la finestra, fa freddo.
items.en: Do not open the window, it is cold.
items.note: negative tu = non plus the infinitive

items.hr: Andiamo al parco! Venite con noi!
items.en: Let's go to the park! Come with us!
items.note: noi and voi imperatives look like the present tense

items.hr: Dammi la mano e dimmi tutto.
items.en: Give me your hand and tell me everything.
items.note: da' + mi = dammi, di' + mi = dimmi, with a double consonant

activities.title: Dare istruzioni
activities.intro: Practise the informal imperative in positive and negative sentences.

questions.prompt: Which is the informal tu imperative of 'scrivere'?
questions.explanation: An -ere verb takes -i in the tu imperative, which looks like the present tu form.

questions.prompt: How do you tell a friend not to smoke?
questions.explanation: A negative order to one person uses non plus the bare infinitive.

questions.prompt: Complete with the informal command that tells a friend to wait: ___ un momento, arrivo subito!
questions.explanation: An -are verb ends in -a in the tu imperative.

questions.prompt: Complete with the noi form that means 'let's go': ___ a mangiare qualcosa!
questions.explanation: The noi imperative is identical to the present tense noi form.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'Close the door, please!'
questions.explanation: Chiudere is an -ere verb, so the tu imperative ends in -i.

activities.title: Consigli a un amico
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A friend is nervous before a job interview, so give advice with informal imperatives.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Domani ho un colloquio e sono nervoso.
lines.en: Tomorrow I have an interview and I am nervous.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Stai tranquillo e dormi almeno otto ore stanotte.
lines.en: Stay calm and sleep at least eight hours tonight.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: E come mi vesto?
lines.en: And how should I dress?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Metti la camicia azzurra, ma non mettere la cravatta.
lines.en: Wear the light blue shirt, but do not wear a tie.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Devo portare qualcosa?
lines.en: Should I bring anything?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Porta due copie del curriculum e arriva dieci minuti prima.
lines.en: Bring two copies of your CV and arrive ten minutes early.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Va bene. Ti chiamo dopo?
lines.en: All right. Shall I call you afterwards?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Chiamami subito e dimmi com'è andata!
lines.en: Call me straight away and tell me how it went!

days.title: L'imperativo formale e il Lei
days.objective: Use the fixed polite forms Scusi, Senta, Prenda, Entri and Mi dia with strangers and in shops.
days.paretoFocus: In Italy you address every stranger with Lei, so these few polite words are the difference between sounding respectful and sounding rude.
drills: Practise Scusi and Senta out loud until they come automatically instead of Scusa and Senti.
Write four requests in a shop using a polite Lei form.
Convert three informal commands you already know into their polite versions.

items: What is the polite version of Scusa?
Where does the pronoun go with a polite Lei form?
What ending do -ere and -ire verbs take in the Lei form?

activities.title: Le forme di cortesia con il Lei
activities.intro: The polite command form is historically the congiuntivo, and you will study that mood properly later, so for now learn these words as a small fixed set of ready-made polite phrases. An -are verb takes -i (Scusi, Entri) and an -ere or -ire verb takes -a (Prenda, Senta). Unlike the informal imperative, the pronoun goes before the verb, never attached to it.

items.hr: Scusi, dov'è la stazione?
items.en: Excuse me, where is the station?
items.note: Scusi is the polite version of scusa

items.hr: Senta, per favore, quanto costa?
items.en: Excuse me, please, how much is it?
items.note: Senta gets a stranger's attention politely

items.hr: Prenda la prima strada a destra.
items.en: Take the first street on the right.
items.note: prendere -> Prenda, with -a for -ere verbs

items.hr: Mi dia un chilo di pane, per favore.
items.en: Give me a kilo of bread, please.
items.note: dare -> dia, and the pronoun mi comes before the verb

items.hr: Non si preoccupi, c'è tempo.
items.en: Do not worry, there is time.
items.note: reflexive si also comes before the polite form

activities.title: Parlare con il Lei
activities.intro: Practise the polite forms used with strangers.

questions.prompt: How do you politely get a stranger's attention to apologise?
questions.explanation: Scusa is for friends, while the polite form of an -are verb ends in -i.

questions.prompt: Choose the polite Lei form of 'prendere': ___ un caffè con noi.
questions.explanation: An -ere verb takes -a in the polite form, while Prendi is informal and Prende is the plain present.

questions.prompt: Complete with the polite Lei form of 'entrare': ___ pure, si accomodi!
questions.explanation: Verbs in -are switch their vowel to -i in the polite form.

questions.prompt: Complete with the polite Lei form of 'dare', placed after the pronoun: Mi ___ il Suo documento, per favore.
questions.explanation: Dare has the short polite form used after a pronoun that stays in front of the verb.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'Excuse me, tell me the truth.' (polite)
questions.explanation: With the polite form the pronoun stands before the verb, unlike dimmi in the informal register.

activities.title: Alla reception dell'albergo
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You are a hotel guest speaking with the receptionist, so keep everything in the polite Lei register.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Buonasera, mi dica pure.
lines.en: Good evening, how can I help you?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Senta, ho una prenotazione a nome Rossi.
lines.en: Excuse me, I have a reservation under the name Rossi.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Un momento. Mi dia un documento, per favore.
lines.en: One moment. Give me an identity document, please.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Eccolo. Scusi, a che ora è la colazione?
lines.en: Here it is. Excuse me, what time is breakfast?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Dalle sette alle dieci. Prenda l'ascensore in fondo a destra.
lines.en: From seven to ten. Take the lift at the end on the right.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Grazie. Mi scusi ancora, c'è il wifi in camera?
lines.en: Thank you. Excuse me again, is there wifi in the room?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Certo, non si preoccupi, la password è sulla scrivania.
lines.en: Of course, do not worry, the password is on the desk.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Perfetto, grazie mille e buona serata.
lines.en: Perfect, thank you very much and have a good evening.

days.title: Il futuro semplice
days.objective: Form and use the simple future of regular verbs and of the common irregular ones.
days.paretoFocus: Plans, appointments and promises all need the future, and its irregular stems are the same ones you already know from the most frequent verbs.
drills: Write the six future forms of lavorare, prendere and partire.
List the future stems of essere, avere, andare, fare, potere and dovere.
Describe your next month in five future sentences.

items: What happens to the -a- of -are verbs in the future?
Which future forms carry a written accent?
How do you say 'we will leave at eight'?

activities.title: Il futuro semplice
activities.intro: The future endings are the same for all three conjugations: -ò, -ai, -à, -emo, -ete, -anno. Verbs in -are change their vowel to e, so lavorare gives lavorerò, while -ere and -ire keep theirs. Several very common verbs lose a vowel in the stem: sarò, avrò, andrò, farò, potrò, dovrò, vorrò, verrò.

items.hr: Domani lavorerò fino alle sei.
items.en: Tomorrow I will work until six.
items.note: lavorare -> lavorerò, the -a- becomes -e-

items.hr: L'anno prossimo prenderemo una casa più grande.
items.en: Next year we will take a bigger house.
items.note: prendere -> prenderò

items.hr: Partiremo alle otto di mattina.
items.en: We will leave at eight in the morning.
items.note: partire -> partirò, the -i- stays

items.hr: Sarò a casa alle sette e avrò tempo per te.
items.en: I will be home at seven and I will have time for you.
items.note: essere -> sarò, avere -> avrò

items.hr: Andremo in Sicilia e faremo il bagno ogni giorno.
items.en: We will go to Sicily and we will swim every day.
items.note: andare -> andrò, fare -> farò

activities.title: Parlare del futuro
activities.intro: Practise regular and irregular future forms.

questions.prompt: Choose the future of 'potere' for lui: Domani non ___ venire.
questions.explanation: Potere loses the -e- of the stem and the third person singular carries a written accent.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is in the simple future?
options: Fra due settimane cominceremo il corso.
Fra due settimane cominciamo il corso.
La settimana scorsa abbiamo cominciato il corso.
questions.answer: Fra due settimane cominceremo il corso.
questions.explanation: Only the first sentence carries a future ending, the others are present, passato prossimo and imperfect.

questions.prompt: Complete with the future of 'essere': Il mese prossimo io ___ in Italia.
questions.explanation: Essere has the short future stem sar- plus the accented first person ending.

questions.prompt: Complete with the future of 'andare': Loro ___ all'università a settembre.
questions.explanation: Andare drops the vowel of the stem and the third person plural ending is -anno.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'Tomorrow we will eat at my parents' house.'
questions.explanation: Mangiare loses the -i- before the future ending, giving mangeremo.

activities.title: Progetti per l'estate
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A friend asks about your summer plans, so answer in the simple future.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Che cosa farai quest'estate?
lines.en: What will you do this summer?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Andrò in Puglia con mia sorella per due settimane.
lines.en: I will go to Puglia with my sister for two weeks.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Quando partirete?
lines.en: When will you leave?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Partiremo il primo agosto e torneremo a metà mese.
lines.en: We will leave on the first of August and come back mid month.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Dove dormirete?
lines.en: Where will you sleep?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Staremo da un'amica, così non spenderemo molto.
lines.en: We will stay at a friend's place, so we will not spend much.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Avrai tempo per vedere Lecce?
lines.en: Will you have time to see Lecce?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sì, ci passeremo due giorni e visiteremo il centro storico.
lines.en: Yes, we will spend two days there and visit the old town.

days.title: Il futuro per le previsioni e le supposizioni
days.objective: Use the future for weather forecasts and for guessing about something happening right now.
days.paretoFocus: Italians constantly use the future to guess, so understanding 'sarà a casa' as 'he is probably at home' unlocks a lot of everyday speech.
drills: Write a three sentence weather forecast for tomorrow in your region.
Guess the age of three people you know using the future of avere.
Answer three 'where is he?' questions with a guess instead of a fact.

items: What does 'Sarà in ufficio' mean about the present moment?
Which tense do weather forecasts use?
How do you guess that someone is about fifty years old?

activities.title: Previsioni e supposizioni
activities.intro: Besides real plans, the future has a second job: it expresses a guess about what is probably true now. Weather forecasts also use it throughout. Verbs in -care and -gare add an h before the future ending to keep the hard sound.

items.hr: Domani ci sarà il sole su tutta la città.
items.en: Tomorrow there will be sun over the whole city.
items.note: forecasts use the future, and città keeps its accent

items.hr: In montagna farà freddo e nevicherà.
items.en: In the mountains it will be cold and it will snow.
items.note: nevicare -> nevicherà, with h to keep the hard c

items.hr: Dove sarà Marco? Sarà ancora in ufficio.
items.en: Where can Marco be? He is probably still at the office.
items.note: here the future talks about right now, not about tomorrow

items.hr: Quanti anni avrà la tua insegnante? Avrà cinquant'anni.
items.en: How old can your teacher be? She is probably fifty.
items.note: avrà means 'she is probably ... years old'

items.hr: Suonano alla porta: sarà il postino.
items.en: Someone is ringing the door: it must be the postman.
items.note: the everyday way of guessing out loud

activities.title: Che cosa succederà?
activities.intro: Practise the future used for forecasts and for guesses about the present.

questions.prompt: The bell rings and you guess it is your sister. What do you say?
questions.explanation: The future of essere expresses a guess about who is at the door right now.

questions.prompt: Choose the correct forecast form: Domenica ___ su tutta la regione.
questions.explanation: Piovere is an -ere verb, so the stem keeps its e before the future ending.

questions.prompt: Complete with the future of 'avere' used as a guess: Non so quanti anni ha, ma ___ almeno sessant'anni.
questions.explanation: The short stem avr- plus the accented third person ending expresses probability.

questions.prompt: Complete with the future of 'fare' in a forecast: In pianura ___ molto caldo.
questions.explanation: Fare has the short future stem far- and the third person ending carries an accent.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'Where can my keys be?'
questions.explanation: Chiavi is plural, so the guessing future of essere is saranno.

activities.title: Che tempo farà domani?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You and a colleague guess about the weather and about a missing person, so use the future for both.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Secondo te, che tempo farà domani?
lines.en: In your opinion, what will the weather be like tomorrow?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Farà freddo di mattina, ma nel pomeriggio ci sarà il sole.
lines.en: It will be cold in the morning, but in the afternoon there will be sun.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Pioverà sul lago?
lines.en: Will it rain over the lake?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Forse pioverà un po', quindi porteremo l'ombrello.
lines.en: Maybe it will rain a little, so we will bring an umbrella.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: A proposito, dov'è Giulia? Non risponde.
lines.en: By the way, where is Giulia? She is not answering.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sarà ancora in riunione, oppure sarà in macchina.
lines.en: She must still be in a meeting, or she must be in the car.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Quanto ci vorrà per arrivare qui?
lines.en: How long will it take to get here?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ci vorranno venti minuti, il traffico sarà tranquillo a quest'ora.
lines.en: It will take twenty minutes, the traffic is probably light at this hour.

days.title: Il condizionale presente
days.objective: Form the present conditional and use it for wishes, soft advice and possibilities.
days.paretoFocus: Vorrei alone will improve every request you make in Italy, and the same endings cover advice and hypothetical wishes.
drills: Write the conditional of volere, potere, dovere and essere for io and tu.
Turn three direct wishes into conditional ones.
Give three pieces of advice with dovresti.

items: Which stems does the conditional share with the future?
How do you say 'I would like to visit Sicily'?
Which conditional form gives advice to a friend?

activities.title: Il condizionale presente
activities.intro: The conditional uses exactly the same stems as the future, with the endings -ei, -esti, -ebbe, -emmo, -este, -ebbero. It softens a wish into a request, turns an order into advice and expresses what would be nice. Note the double consonants in -ebbe and -emmo.

items.hr: Vorrei un caffè, per favore.
items.en: I would like a coffee, please.
items.note: volere -> vorrei, far politer than voglio

items.hr: Mi piacerebbe visitare la Sicilia.
items.en: I would like to visit Sicily.
items.note: mi piacerebbe expresses a wish about something you have not done

items.hr: Potresti aiutarmi un momento?
items.en: Could you help me for a moment?
items.note: potere -> potrei, potresti

items.hr: Dovremmo partire prima delle otto.
items.en: We should leave before eight.
items.note: dovere -> dovrei: advice, not obligation

items.hr: Sarebbe bello vederci sabato.
items.en: It would be nice to meet on Saturday.
items.note: essere -> sarei, saresti, sarebbe

activities.title: Desideri e consigli
activities.intro: Practise conditional forms for wishes and advice.

questions.prompt: Which sentence politely asks for a ticket at a counter?
questions.explanation: The conditional of volere is the standard polite way to ask for something.

questions.prompt: Choose the conditional of 'potere' for tu.
questions.explanation: The conditional keeps the short stem potr- and adds the ending -esti.

questions.prompt: Complete with the conditional of 'dovere' to give advice: Tu ___ dormire di più.
questions.explanation: The short stem dovr- plus -esti turns an obligation into a suggestion.

questions.prompt: Complete with the conditional of 'essere' meaning 'would it be': ___ possibile cambiare l'appuntamento?
questions.explanation: The third person conditional of essere is written with a double b.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'I would like to live by the sea.'
questions.explanation: Piacere works with an indirect pronoun, and the infinitive follows the conditional.

activities.title: Un invito per sabato
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A friend proposes an outing, so answer with wishes and advice in the conditional.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ti piacerebbe venire al lago sabato?
lines.en: Would you like to come to the lake on Saturday?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Mi piacerebbe molto, ma dovrei finire un lavoro.
lines.en: I would like that a lot, but I should finish a piece of work.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Potresti lavorare venerdì sera?
lines.en: Could you work on Friday evening?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sì, sarebbe perfetto, così sabato sarei libero.
lines.en: Yes, that would be perfect, so on Saturday I would be free.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Allora partiremmo alle nove. Ti va bene?
lines.en: Then we would leave at nine. Is that fine with you?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Vorrei partire un po' prima, se per te è lo stesso.
lines.en: I would like to leave a bit earlier, if it is the same for you.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Va bene. Dovremmo portare qualcosa da mangiare?
lines.en: All right. Should we bring something to eat?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Io porterei del pane e del formaggio, tu potresti portare la frutta.
lines.en: I would bring bread and cheese, you could bring the fruit.

days.title: Chiedere con cortesia
days.objective: Make polite requests by combining the conditional with the Lei register.
days.paretoFocus: Getting a request right the first time, in a shop, a hotel or an office, saves you every awkward moment abroad.
drills: Write five requests you would need on a real trip, all with a conditional.
Practise softening three direct orders into polite questions.
Say a full polite exchange: request, thanks, and closing.

items: Which two words open almost any polite request to a stranger?
How do you ask someone to repeat what they said, politely?
What does 'Le dispiacerebbe' mean?

activities.title: Chiedere con gentilezza
activities.intro: The politest requests put a conditional inside the Lei register. Scusi or Senta opens the exchange, then potrebbe or vorrei plus an infinitive carries the request. Le dispiacerebbe is the softest of all and is used when you are asking a real favour.

items.hr: Scusi, mi potrebbe dire dov'è la fermata?
items.en: Excuse me, could you tell me where the stop is?
items.note: potrebbe plus an infinitive is very polite

items.hr: Vorrei prenotare un tavolo per due, per favore.
items.en: I would like to book a table for two, please.
items.note: vorrei softens any request

items.hr: Le dispiacerebbe aprire la finestra?
items.en: Would you mind opening the window?
items.note: Le dispiacerebbe means 'would you mind'

items.hr: Mi darebbe un bicchiere d'acqua?
items.en: Would you give me a glass of water?
items.note: conditional plus Lei is softer than the polite imperative

items.hr: Grazie mille, è molto gentile.
items.en: Thank you very much, that is very kind.
items.note: the standard way to close a polite request

activities.title: Richieste cortesi
activities.intro: Practise choosing the most appropriate polite request.

questions.prompt: Which request is the most polite when speaking to a stranger?
questions.explanation: The conditional plus the Lei form plus per favore is the softest combination.

questions.prompt: Choose the polite way to ask a shop assistant about the price.
questions.explanation: With a stranger you need the Lei form, so Scusa and Dimmi are too familiar.

questions.prompt: Complete with the polite conditional of 'potere' for Lei: ___ ripetere, per favore?
questions.explanation: The third person conditional carries a double b and works as a Lei form.

questions.prompt: Complete with the polite conditional of 'volere' for io: ___ una pizza margherita.
questions.explanation: Ordering with the conditional instead of voglio is the normal register in a restaurant.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'Excuse me, would you mind waiting a moment?' (polite)
questions.explanation: The pronoun Le comes before the conditional, and the infinitive follows it.

activities.title: Allo sportello
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You are at an information desk asking for help, so keep every request polite.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Buongiorno, prego.
lines.en: Good morning, go ahead.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Buongiorno, vorrei un'informazione sui treni per Firenze.
lines.en: Good morning, I would like some information about trains to Florence.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Certo. Per quando?
lines.en: Of course. For when?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Per domani mattina. Mi potrebbe dire a che ora parte il primo?
lines.en: For tomorrow morning. Could you tell me what time the first one leaves?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Alle sei e dieci, dal binario tre.
lines.en: At ten past six, from platform three.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Grazie. Le dispiacerebbe scrivermi l'orario su un foglio?
lines.en: Thank you. Would you mind writing the time down for me on a piece of paper?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Volentieri. Ecco a Lei.
lines.en: Gladly. Here you are.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Grazie mille, è molto gentile. Buona giornata.
lines.en: Thank you very much, that is very kind. Have a good day.

days.title: Il comparativo
days.objective: Compare two things with più di, meno di, più che and come, and use migliore and peggiore.
days.paretoFocus: Comparing prices, places, food and options is one of the most frequent things you do in a foreign language.
drills: Compare two cities you know in three sentences with di.
Write two comparisons that need che instead of di and explain why.
Compare two restaurants using migliore and peggiore.

items: When do you use che instead of di in a comparison?
How do you say two things are equally good?
What is the irregular comparative of buono?

activities.title: Più, meno, come
activities.intro: Use più or meno plus di before a noun, a name or a pronoun, and remember that di joins with the article (del, della, dell'). Use che when you compare two adjectives, two infinitives, two nouns for the same subject, or when a preposition follows. For equality use come or quanto.

items.hr: Milano è più grande di Bologna.
items.en: Milan is bigger than Bologna.
items.note: più ... di before a name

items.hr: Questo film è meno interessante dell'altro.
items.en: This film is less interesting than the other one.
items.note: di plus l'altro gives dell'altro

items.hr: È più facile leggere che scrivere.
items.en: It is easier to read than to write.
items.note: che between two infinitives

items.hr: Mangio più frutta che verdura.
items.en: I eat more fruit than vegetables.
items.note: che when the same subject compares two nouns

items.hr: Il caffè qui è buono come a casa mia.
items.en: The coffee here is as good as at my place.
items.note: come or quanto for equality, and buono becomes migliore for 'better'

activities.title: Fare paragoni
activities.intro: Practise choosing between di, che and come.

questions.prompt: Choose the right word: Anna è più alta ___ sua sorella.
questions.explanation: Two different people compared for one quality take di.

questions.prompt: Choose the right word: È più comodo andare in treno ___ prendere l'aereo.
questions.explanation: When two infinitives are compared, the link word is che.

questions.prompt: Complete with the word that expresses equality: Luca è alto ___ suo fratello.
questions.explanation: Equality between two people is expressed with come or with quanto.

questions.prompt: Complete with the irregular comparative that means 'better': Questa pizza è ___ di quella di ieri.
questions.explanation: Buono has an irregular comparative that replaces più buono in careful Italian.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'The winter here is colder than in Rome.'
questions.explanation: A preposition follows the comparison, so the link word must be che.

activities.title: Due città a confronto
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A friend is deciding where to move, so compare the two options in every answer.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Secondo te, è meglio Torino o Bologna?
lines.en: In your opinion, is Turin or Bologna better?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Torino è più grande di Bologna, ma è anche meno tranquilla.
lines.en: Turin is bigger than Bologna, but it is also less quiet.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: E gli affitti?
lines.en: And the rents?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Gli affitti a Bologna sono più cari che a Torino.
lines.en: Rents in Bologna are more expensive than in Turin.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Il cibo com'è?
lines.en: What is the food like?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Si mangia bene in tutte e due, il cibo è buono come a casa mia.
lines.en: You eat well in both, the food is as good as at my place.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Quindi che cosa scelgo?
lines.en: So what should I choose?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Per il lavoro Torino è migliore, per la vita di tutti i giorni preferisco Bologna.
lines.en: For work Turin is better, for everyday life I prefer Bologna.

days.title: Il superlativo
days.objective: Say that something is the most or the least of a group, and form the absolute superlative in -issimo.
days.paretoFocus: The -issimo ending is the fastest way to sound enthusiastic in Italian, and the relative superlative is needed for any recommendation.
drills: Name the best and the worst thing in three categories you care about.
Turn five adjectives you know into their -issimo form, checking the spelling.
Write three recommendations using il più plus an adjective.

items: Which preposition follows a relative superlative?
How do you turn buono into a strong absolute superlative?
Why does lungo become lunghissimo?

activities.title: Il più e il meno
activities.intro: The relative superlative is article plus più or meno plus adjective, followed by di for the group. The absolute superlative is formed by dropping the final vowel of the adjective and adding -issimo, which then agrees like any adjective. Words in -co and -go add an h to keep the hard sound.

items.hr: È il ristorante più famoso della città.
items.en: It is the most famous restaurant in the city.
items.note: article plus più plus adjective, then di plus the group

items.hr: Sono le scarpe meno care del negozio.
items.en: They are the least expensive shoes in the shop.
items.note: the article agrees with the noun, here feminine plural

items.hr: Questo gelato è buonissimo!
items.en: This ice cream is really delicious!
items.note: buono -> buonissimo, the absolute superlative

items.hr: Il viaggio è stato lunghissimo.
items.en: The journey was extremely long.
items.note: lungo -> lunghissimo, with h to keep the hard g

items.hr: Marco è il migliore della classe.
items.en: Marco is the best in the class.
items.note: migliore and peggiore also work as superlatives with the article

activities.title: Il migliore di tutti
activities.intro: Practise both kinds of superlative.

questions.prompt: Choose the correct absolute superlative of 'simpatico'.
questions.explanation: Simpatico is stressed early and keeps the soft c, so no h is added before the ending.

questions.prompt: Which sentence contains a relative superlative?
options: È la piazza più bella della città.
La piazza è più bella di prima.
questions.answer: È la piazza più bella della città.
questions.explanation: Only the first sentence has an article plus più plus a group introduced by di.

questions.prompt: Complete with the absolute superlative of 'caro': Quel cappotto è ___, non lo compro.
questions.explanation: Drop the final vowel of the adjective and add the masculine singular ending.

questions.prompt: Complete with the preposition and article that follow a relative superlative: È il museo più visitato ___ regione.
questions.explanation: Di plus la gives one written word before a feminine singular noun.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'It is the longest river in Italy.'
questions.explanation: The group is introduced by di, which shortens to d' before the vowel of Italia.

activities.title: Il posto più bello
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A friend asks for recommendations, so answer with superlatives.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Qual è il posto più bello della tua città?
lines.en: What is the most beautiful place in your city?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Il parco vicino al fiume, soprattutto la sera. È tranquillissimo.
lines.en: The park near the river, especially in the evening. It is extremely quiet.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: E dove si mangia meglio?
lines.en: And where do you eat best?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Nella trattoria in centro: fanno la pasta migliore del quartiere.
lines.en: In the trattoria downtown: they make the best pasta in the neighbourhood.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: È molto cara?
lines.en: Is it very expensive?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: No, è il posto meno caro della zona, e le porzioni sono grandissime.
lines.en: No, it is the least expensive place in the area, and the portions are huge.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: E il museo? Vale la pena?
lines.en: And the museum? Is it worth it?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: È il museo più visitato della regione, ma la domenica è affollatissimo.
lines.en: It is the most visited museum in the region, but on Sundays it is packed.

days.title: Gli avverbi in -mente
days.objective: Form adverbs from adjectives with -mente and place them correctly in the sentence.
days.paretoFocus: One simple rule turns hundreds of adjectives you already know into adverbs, which multiplies what you can express.
drills: Turn ten adjectives you know into adverbs and check the ones ending in -le and -re.
Write three sentences that open with an adverb in -mente.
Say three sentences using bene and male correctly instead of an adjective.

items: Which form of the adjective do you start from?
What happens to adjectives ending in -le or -re?
What is the adverb of buono?

activities.title: Gli avverbi in -mente
activities.intro: Take the feminine singular of the adjective and add -mente: lento gives lentamente. If the adjective ends in -le or -re after a consonant, drop the final e first: facile gives facilmente, regolare gives regolarmente. Buono and cattivo do not follow the rule, since their adverbs are bene and male.

items.hr: Parla lentamente, per favore.
items.en: Speak slowly, please.
items.note: lento -> lenta -> lentamente

items.hr: Vado regolarmente in palestra.
items.en: I go to the gym regularly.
items.note: regolare loses its final e before -mente

items.hr: Fortunatamente non è successo niente.
items.en: Fortunately nothing happened.
items.note: adverbs in -mente often open the sentence

items.hr: Ho capito facilmente la spiegazione.
items.en: I understood the explanation easily.
items.note: facile -> facilmente

items.hr: Suona il pianoforte molto bene.
items.en: He plays the piano very well.
items.note: bene and male are irregular and never take -mente

activities.title: Dagli aggettivi agli avverbi
activities.intro: Practise building adverbs and choosing between an adjective and an adverb.

questions.prompt: Choose the adverb built from 'veloce'.
questions.explanation: Veloce ends in -ce, not in -le or -re, so it keeps its final e before the ending.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses the correct word after the verb?
questions.explanation: A verb needs an adverb, and the adverb matching buono is the irregular form bene.

questions.prompt: Form the adverb from the adjective 'raro': Vado ___ al cinema.
questions.explanation: Start from the feminine singular of the adjective and add the ending.

questions.prompt: Form the adverb from the adjective 'gentile': Mi ha risposto ___ al telefono.
questions.explanation: Adjectives ending in -le drop the final e before the ending.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'Unfortunately I cannot come tonight.'
questions.explanation: The adverb opens the sentence and comments on the whole statement that follows.

activities.title: Come studi l'italiano?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A classmate asks how you study, so use an adverb in -mente in each answer.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Studi italiano tutti i giorni?
lines.en: Do you study Italian every day?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Studio regolarmente, venti minuti la mattina presto.
lines.en: I study regularly, twenty minutes early in the morning.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: E capisci quando parlano in televisione?
lines.en: And do you understand when they speak on television?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Non sempre, ma se parlano lentamente capisco quasi tutto.
lines.en: Not always, but if they speak slowly I understand almost everything.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Che cosa è la parte più difficile?
lines.en: What is the hardest part?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: I pronomi. Purtroppo li sbaglio ancora frequentemente.
lines.en: The pronouns. Unfortunately I still get them wrong frequently.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Anche io! Però parli molto bene.
lines.en: Me too! But you speak very well.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Grazie, sinceramente non me lo aspettavo.
lines.en: Thanks, honestly I was not expecting that.

days.title: Il che relativo
days.objective: Join two ideas into one sentence with the relative che, used both as subject and as direct object, and never drop it the way English does.
days.paretoFocus: Che is by far the most frequent relative in spoken Italian and it is what turns short beginner sentences into adult-sounding ones, while forgetting it produces an error every native speaker notices immediately.
drills: Take five pairs of short sentences you can already say and join each pair with che.
Translate five English sentences that drop 'that' or 'which', for example 'the film I saw', and check that your Italian keeps che.
Describe three people you know using a che clause for each one.

items: Does che change for gender or number?
Which English habit must you resist when translating 'the book I read'?
What kind of relative pronoun do you need after a preposition instead of che?

activities.title: Una sola parola per unire
activities.intro: Che is the all-purpose relative pronoun: it means 'who', 'whom', 'that' or 'which' and it never changes for gender or number. It works both when the noun is the subject of the second verb and when it is the direct object. English can leave it out ('the book I read'), but Italian NEVER can, so che must always be there.

items.hr: Il libro che leggo è molto interessante.
items.en: The book I am reading is very interesting.
items.note: English drops 'that' here, Italian cannot

items.hr: La ragazza che parla con Marco è mia sorella.
items.en: The girl who is talking to Marco is my sister.
items.note: here che is the subject of parla

items.hr: I film che abbiamo visto ieri sera erano bellissimi.
items.en: The films we saw last night were wonderful.
items.note: che stays identical with a masculine plural noun

items.hr: Ecco le fotografie che ho fatto in vacanza.
items.en: Here are the photos I took on holiday.
items.note: che is the direct object of ho fatto

items.hr: Non trovo più l'ombrello che mi hai regalato.
items.en: I cannot find the umbrella you gave me any more.
items.note: after a preposition (with, about, for) you need cui instead of che, which comes later

activities.title: Unire le frasi
activities.intro: Practise the relative che and revise the past and the conditional.

questions.prompt: Which is the correct Italian for 'the film I saw yesterday'?
options: il film che ho visto ieri
il film quale ho visto ieri
il film il che ho visto ieri
questions.answer: il film che ho visto ieri
questions.explanation: Italian must keep the relative pronoun even where English drops it, and the everyday relative is che.

questions.prompt: Completa: Da bambino ___ sempre in bicicletta.
questions.explanation: A repeated habit in the past takes the imperfetto, so andavo. Da bambino signals an ongoing childhood routine, not a single finished event.

questions.prompt: Completa: La signora ___ abita sopra di noi è molto gentile.
questions.explanation: You need the invariable relative pronoun linking la signora to the verb abita, of which it is the subject.

questions.prompt: Completa con il condizionale presente di volere: ___ un caffè, per favore.
questions.explanation: Volere has an irregular conditional stem vorr-, so the first person is vorrei, the standard polite way to order something.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'The girl who works here is my friend.'
questions.explanation: Che follows the noun it refers to and comes straight before the verb of the relative clause; amica is not a family word, so the possessive keeps its article.

activities.title: Chi è quella persona?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You are at a party and your friend keeps asking who people are, so every answer needs a che clause.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Chi è la ragazza che parla con tuo fratello?
lines.en: Who is the girl who is talking to your brother?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: È Giulia, l'amica che lavora con me in ufficio.
lines.en: It is Giulia, the friend who works with me at the office.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: E il ragazzo che sta accanto a lei?
lines.en: And the boy who is standing next to her?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: È il tipo che ci ha aiutato quando abbiamo traslocato.
lines.en: He is the guy who helped us when we moved house.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ah, quello che ha la macchina rossa?
lines.en: Ah, the one who has the red car?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Esatto. Le persone che vedi qui sono quasi tutte colleghe.
lines.en: Exactly. The people you see here are nearly all colleagues.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Allora la festa che avete organizzato è riuscita davvero bene.
lines.en: So the party you organised has turned out really well.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sì, ogni anno facciamo una festa che piace a tutti.
lines.en: Yes, every year we throw a party that everyone likes.

days.title: Alla posta e le spedizioni
days.objective: Handle a whole post office counter transaction in the Lei form: sending a parcel, choosing registered post, filling in a form, getting a receipt and paying a bill.
days.paretoFocus: Sending and collecting parcels is one of the few errands where you cannot guess your way through, and in Italy the post office is also where people pay bills, which no one expects the first time.
drills: Say out loud, in the Lei form, the three sentences you would need to send a two kilo parcel abroad.
Write the counter clerk's four most likely questions and your answers to each.
List eight post office words with their correct article and cover the English to test yourself.

items: How do you ask for registered post rather than ordinary post?
Which document do you keep so that you can follow the parcel afterwards?
What do you say when you go to collect a registered letter?

activities.title: Allo sportello
activities.intro: At the counter you use the Lei form throughout, and the clerk will use it back. The core verbs are spedire (to send), ritirare (to collect), pesare (to weigh) and compilare (to fill in a form). Note that in Italy the post office also takes payments for household bills, which is why the queue is often long.

items.hr: Buongiorno, vorrei spedire questo pacco in Francia.
items.en: Good morning, I would like to send this parcel to France.
items.note: spedire is an -isc- verb: spedisco, spedisci, spedisce

items.hr: Preferisce una raccomandata o una spedizione ordinaria?
items.en: Do you prefer registered post or ordinary post?
items.note: la raccomandata costs more but gives you proof of delivery

items.hr: Per una lettera basta un francobollo, ma il pacco lo dobbiamo pesare.
items.en: For a letter a stamp is enough, but we have to weigh the parcel.
items.note: il francobollo, il pacco, la busta
