activities.intro: Practise choosing the fixed expression that fits each occasion.

questions.prompt: A friend has an exam tomorrow. What do you say?
questions.explanation: In bocca al lupo is the standard wish before a test or a challenge.

questions.prompt: Someone says in bocca al lupo to you. What is the expected reply?
questions.explanation: The traditional answer is crepi, short for crepi il lupo. Saying only grazie is considered bad luck by many.

questions.prompt: Complete the birthday wish: 'Cento di questi ______!'
questions.explanation: The fixed phrase wishes the person a hundred more days like this one.

questions.prompt: Complete the formal expression of sympathy: 'Le porgo le mie sentite ______.'
questions.explanation: Condoglianze is always plural and always feminine in this expression.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'Best wishes for a merry Christmas and a happy new year.'
questions.explanation: Auguri di introduces the occasion, and buon and felice come before their nouns.

activities.title: Tanti auguri!
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You wish a colleague well for a birthday and for an exam.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Oggi è il mio compleanno, lo sapevi?
lines.en: Today is my birthday, did you know?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: No! Tanti auguri, cento di questi giorni!
lines.en: No! Happy birthday, many happy returns!

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Grazie! Lo festeggio domani sera con pochi amici.
lines.en: Thank you! I am celebrating it tomorrow evening with a few friends.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Bello. E per l'esame di giovedì, in bocca al lupo.
lines.en: Nice. And for Thursday's exam, good luck.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Crepi! Ne ho proprio bisogno.
lines.en: Thanks! I really need it.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Andrà benissimo, hai studiato tantissimo.
lines.en: It will go very well, you have studied a huge amount.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Speriamo. Ci sentiamo dopo l'esame.
lines.en: Let us hope so. We will talk after the exam.

days.title: Le feste e le tradizioni italiane
days.objective: Name the main Italian public holidays with their dates and say what people do on them.
days.paretoFocus: Holidays decide when offices, shops and transport are closed, and Ferragosto in particular reshapes daily life for half of August.
drills: Say five Italian holidays with their dates, using il plus the number and the month.
Write one sentence about what people typically do on each of three of them.
Plan an imaginary week in mid August and note which days would be difficult.

items: Which date is Ferragosto and what happens in the cities around it?
What is the Monday after Easter called and what do people do on it?
Which two dates in December are public holidays in Italy?

activities.title: Il calendario delle feste
activities.intro: Italian dates take il plus the number plus the month, with no preposition before the month. The first of the month is the only one that uses an ordinal. These five holidays are the ones that most affect everyday plans.

items.hr: Il 6 gennaio, per l'Epifania, la Befana porta i dolci ai bambini.
items.en: On 6 January, for Epiphany, the Befana brings sweets to the children.
items.note: the Befana is an old woman on a broom, not a saint

items.hr: Il lunedì dopo Pasqua si chiama Pasquetta e molti fanno una gita fuori città.
items.en: The Monday after Easter is called Pasquetta and many people take a trip out of town.
items.note: lunedì with the final accent; città is invariable

items.hr: Il 2 giugno è la Festa della Repubblica ed è festivo in tutta Italia.
items.en: The 2nd of June is Republic Day and it is a public holiday all over Italy.
items.note: ed instead of e before a word starting with e

items.hr: A Ferragosto, il 15 agosto, molte città si svuotano e parecchi negozi restano chiusi.
items.en: At Ferragosto, on 15 August, many cities empty out and quite a few shops stay closed.
items.note: the whole middle of August is quiet, not only the 15th

items.hr: Il 26 dicembre, Santo Stefano, è ancora festa e di solito si passa in famiglia.
items.en: The 26th of December, Saint Stephen's day, is still a holiday and is usually spent with the family.
items.note: Italy has two consecutive Christmas holidays, the 25th and the 26th

activities.title: Quando si festeggia
activities.intro: Practise the dates and the customs of the main Italian holidays.

questions.prompt: On which date is Ferragosto?
questions.explanation: Ferragosto falls on 15 August, and around it many city businesses close for their annual break.

questions.prompt: What is the Monday after Easter called in Italy?
questions.explanation: Pasquetta, also called lunedì dell'Angelo, is a public holiday traditionally spent outdoors.

questions.prompt: Complete the date of Republic Day: 'La Festa della Repubblica è il ______ giugno.' (write the figure)
questions.explanation: Italian dates use cardinal numbers, so il 2 giugno, with no ordinal except for the first of the month.

questions.prompt: Complete with the figure of the December holiday that follows Christmas Day: 'Il ______ dicembre è Santo Stefano.'
questions.explanation: Santo Stefano falls on 26 December and is a full public holiday in Italy.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'On the mid August public holiday many shops stay closed.'
questions.explanation: Restare works like essere here, so the adjective chiusi agrees with negozi.

activities.title: Ad agosto è tutto chiuso
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You are planning a visit in August and a friend explains what happens.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Vuoi venire a Milano la settimana di Ferragosto?
lines.en: Do you want to come to Milan in the week of Ferragosto?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Perché no? Il 15 agosto è festa, giusto?
lines.en: Why not? The 15th of August is a holiday, right?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Sì, ma attento: la città si svuota e molti negozi restano chiusi.
lines.en: Yes, but careful: the city empties out and many shops stay closed.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Anche i ristoranti?
lines.en: The restaurants as well?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Parecchi chiudono per due settimane. Chi resta va al mare o in montagna.
lines.en: Quite a few close for two weeks. Those who stay go to the seaside or the mountains.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Allora vengo a settembre, dopo le vacanze.
lines.en: Then I will come in September, after the holidays.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ottima idea, a settembre la città riparte.
lines.en: Excellent idea, in September the city starts up again.

days.title: Il telefono, internet e i social
days.objective: Talk about phones, connection problems and online messaging in everyday Italian.
days.paretoFocus: Half of all daily communication now happens through a screen, and the Italian words for it are not the English ones learners expect.
drills: Say three things that can go wrong with a phone, each in one sentence.
Describe how you usually contact three different people: family, a friend, an office.
Explain in two sentences what you do online most days.

items: How do you say there is no signal where you are?
Which verb goes with a photo you put online?
How do you say your battery is nearly flat?

activities.title: Non prende il telefono
activities.intro: Italian has its own vocabulary here: il campo is the signal, la rete is the network, and prendere is what a phone does when it has coverage. Note that caricare means to charge a battery and also to upload.

items.hr: Qui il telefono non prende, non c'è campo.
items.en: My phone has no reception here, there is no signal.
items.note: prendere used intransitively for signal; il campo = signal bars

items.hr: Ho la batteria quasi scarica, devo caricare il telefono.
items.en: My battery is nearly flat, I have to charge my phone.
items.note: scarica agrees with la batteria; the opposite is carica

items.hr: Ti mando il link in chat, così lo apri con calma.
items.en: I will send you the link in the chat, so you can open it at your leisure.
items.note: mandare + indirect pronoun; con calma = without rushing

items.hr: Ho pubblicato le foto del viaggio e hanno messo tutti mi piace.
items.en: I posted the trip photos and everyone liked them.
items.note: mettere mi piace is the normal way to say to like a post

items.hr: Non riesco a collegarmi, la connessione è lentissima.
items.en: I cannot connect, the connection is extremely slow.
items.note: riuscire a + infinitive = to manage to

activities.title: Problemi di connessione
activities.intro: Practise talking about phones, signal and going online.

questions.prompt: Which sentence says there is no phone signal?
questions.explanation: Il campo is the mobile signal. Il tempo is time or weather, and il posto is a place or seat.

questions.prompt: Which verb means to manage to do something?
questions.explanation: Riuscire a plus an infinitive means to manage or to be able to in practice, unlike potere which is permission or possibility.

questions.prompt: Make the adjective agree: 'Ho la batteria quasi ______.' (from scarico, meaning flat)
questions.explanation: La batteria is feminine singular, so the adjective is scarica.

questions.prompt: Complete with the io form of the verb meaning to manage: 'Non ______ a collegarmi alla rete.'
questions.explanation: Riuscire is irregular in the present, giving io riesco, and it always takes a before an infinitive.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'I am sending you the web address by message.'
questions.explanation: The indirect pronoun ti comes before the verb, and in chat takes no article.

activities.title: Ti sento male
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. The line is bad and you sort out how to keep in touch.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Pronto? Ti sento malissimo, dove sei?
lines.en: Hello? I can hardly hear you, where are you?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sono in treno, qui il telefono non prende bene.
lines.en: I am on the train, my phone does not get a good signal here.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Allora scrivimi, ti mando l'indirizzo in chat.
lines.en: Then message me, I will send you the address in the chat.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Va bene, ma ho la batteria quasi scarica.
lines.en: All right, but my battery is nearly flat.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Mandami solo un messaggio quando arrivi.
lines.en: Just send me a message when you arrive.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Perfetto. Hai visto le foto che ho pubblicato ieri?
lines.en: Perfect. Did you see the photos I posted yesterday?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Sì, bellissime! Ho messo mi piace a tutte.
lines.en: Yes, gorgeous! I liked every one of them.

days.title: Dire la propria opinione
days.objective: Give an opinion and agree or disagree without needing the subjunctive.
days.paretoFocus: Opinions come up in every conversation, and secondo me plus the ordinary indicative lets you take part fully long before you learn more complex forms.
drills: Give three opinions about films, food or weather, each starting with secondo me.
Agree with one of them and disagree with another, giving a reason each time.
Rewrite one opinion using penso di plus an infinitive.

items: Which two short phrases introduce an opinion and take the ordinary indicative?
How do you say you agree, and how do you say you do not agree at all?
When can you use penso di plus an infinitive?

activities.title: Secondo me
activities.intro: The safest way to give an opinion in Italian is secondo me or per me followed by an ordinary indicative verb. Essere d'accordo, not to be agreed, is the verb for agreeing. When the opinion is about yourself, penso di plus an infinitive works instead.

items.hr: Secondo me questo film è troppo lungo.
items.en: In my opinion this film is too long.
items.note: secondo me + indicativo, never secondo io

items.hr: Per me hai ragione, ma non è così semplice.
items.en: For me you are right, but it is not that simple.
items.note: avere ragione, not essere ragione; così keeps its accent

items.hr: Sono d'accordo con te su questo punto.
items.en: I agree with you on this point.
items.note: essere d'accordo con qualcuno

items.hr: Non sono per niente d'accordo.
items.en: I do not agree at all.
items.note: per niente strengthens a negative

items.hr: Penso di avere ragione, ma forse mi sbaglio.
items.en: I think I am right, but maybe I am wrong.
items.note: penso di + infinitive when both verbs share one subject

activities.title: Sono d'accordo o no
activities.intro: Practise stating an opinion and reacting to someone else's.

questions.prompt: Which sentence correctly introduces your own opinion?
options: Secondo io questo film è noioso.
Secondo me questo film è noioso.
Secondo mio questo film è noioso.
Secondo mi questo film è noioso.
questions.answer: Secondo me questo film è noioso.
questions.explanation: Secondo is a preposition, so it takes the stressed pronoun me, never the subject pronoun io.

questions.prompt: How do you say that someone is right?
questions.explanation: Italian uses avere with ragione and torto, where English uses to be right or wrong.

questions.prompt: Complete the verb of agreement: 'Sono ______ con te, hai spiegato bene.'
questions.explanation: The fixed phrase is essere d'accordo con qualcuno, with the apostrophe on d'.

questions.prompt: Complete with the preposition used when both verbs share one subject: 'Penso ______ avere capito.'
questions.explanation: When the opinion is about the speaker, pensare takes di plus the infinitive.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'I do not agree at all.'
questions.explanation: Per niente sits between the verb and d'accordo and reinforces the non.

activities.title: Non la penso così
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You disagree politely about something you both watched.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Che ne dici del film di ieri sera?
lines.en: What do you think of last night's film?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Secondo me era troppo lungo e non succedeva niente.
lines.en: In my opinion it was too long and nothing happened.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Davvero? A me è piaciuto molto.
lines.en: Really? I liked it a lot.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Capisco, ma non sono per niente d'accordo sul finale.
lines.en: I understand, but I do not agree at all about the ending.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Il finale era la parte migliore!
lines.en: The ending was the best part!

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Su una cosa hai ragione: la musica era bellissima.
lines.en: You are right about one thing: the music was beautiful.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ecco, almeno su quello siamo d'accordo.
lines.en: There you go, at least on that we agree.

days.title: Fare una proposta
days.objective: Suggest a plan, react to someone else's suggestion and settle on one together.
days.paretoFocus: Making plans is where conversations actually go somewhere, and Italian has four short formulas that do almost all the work.
drills: Suggest the same outing four ways, using a different formula each time.
Reject one suggestion and immediately propose an alternative.
End an imaginary planning conversation by fixing a day, a time and a place.

items: Which suggestion formula uses a negative question with the present tense?
Which two formulas are followed by an infinitive?
How do you close a plan by saying let us do it this way?

activities.title: Perché non facciamo così?
activities.intro: Italian suggestions come in four shapes: perché non plus the present, che ne dici di plus an infinitive, potremmo plus an infinitive, and the plain noi imperative. They differ in strength, not in meaning, and all of them expect an answer.

items.hr: Perché non andiamo al mare domenica?
items.en: Why do not we go to the seaside on Sunday?
items.note: perché non + present indicative, the most common suggestion

items.hr: Che ne dici di prendere qualcosa al volo?
items.en: How about grabbing a quick bite?
items.note: che ne dici di + infinitive; al volo = quickly

items.hr: Potremmo invitare anche Luca e Sara.
items.en: We could invite Luca and Sara too.
items.note: potremmo is the noi conditional of potere, softer than possiamo

items.hr: Facciamo così: io compro il pane, tu porti il vino.
items.en: Let us do this: I will buy the bread, you bring the wine.
items.note: facciamo così closes a plan and shares out the jobs

items.hr: Per me va benissimo, decidi tu l'ora.
items.en: That is absolutely fine by me, you decide the time.
items.note: decidi tu with the pronoun after the verb for emphasis

activities.title: Mettiamoci d'accordo
activities.intro: Practise proposing plans and reaching an agreement.

questions.prompt: Which suggestion uses a conditional to sound softer?
questions.explanation: Potremmo is the conditional of potere and presents the plan as one option among several.

questions.prompt: Which reply agrees and hands the decision to the other person?
options: Per me va benissimo, decidi tu.
questions.answer: Per me va benissimo, decidi tu.
questions.explanation: Va benissimo accepts, and decidi tu passes the choice over. The others delay or refuse.

questions.prompt: Complete with the infinitive of the verb meaning to go: 'Che ne dici di ______ a piedi?'
questions.explanation: Che ne dici di is always followed by an infinitive, never by a conjugated verb.

questions.prompt: Complete with the noi conditional of potere: '______ mangiare fuori stasera.' (we could)
questions.explanation: The noi form of the conditional of potere is potremmo, with a single t and a double m.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'Why do not we go to the seaside on Sunday?'
questions.explanation: Perché non takes the ordinary present, and days of the week take no preposition.

activities.title: Che facciamo domenica?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You and a friend propose plans until you settle on one.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Domenica sei libero? Che facciamo?
lines.en: Are you free on Sunday? What shall we do?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Perché non andiamo al mare? Dicono che farà bel tempo.
lines.en: Why do not we go to the seaside? They say the weather will be good.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Domenica al mare c'è troppa gente. Che ne dici di una gita in collina?
lines.en: On Sunday the seaside is too crowded. How about a trip to the hills?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Buona idea. Potremmo invitare anche Luca.
lines.en: Good idea. We could invite Luca too.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Volentieri, lui ha la macchina grande.
lines.en: Gladly, he has the big car.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Facciamo così: io preparo i panini, tu chiami Luca.
lines.en: Let us do this: I will make the sandwiches, you call Luca.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Perfetto. Partiamo alle 9:00?
lines.en: Perfect. Shall we leave at 9:00?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Per me va benissimo, ci vediamo davanti a casa tua.
lines.en: That is absolutely fine by me, see you in front of your house.

days.title: I verbi pronominali frequenti
days.objective: Use the five commonest pronominal verbs: andarsene, farcela, cavarsela, volerci and metterci.
days.paretoFocus: These verbs are everywhere in spoken Italian and cannot be worked out from their parts, so learning them as whole units is the only route.
drills: Say how long three journeys you know take, using ci vogliono or ci vuole correctly.
Say how long you personally take over three daily tasks, using ci metto.
Announce that you are leaving, in three different tenses.

items: What is the difference between ci vuole and ci metto?
How do you say I am leaving using andarsene?
What does non ce la faccio mean and when do you say it?

activities.title: Me ne vado, non ce la faccio
activities.intro: Pronominal verbs glue pronouns onto a verb and change its meaning. Volerci states the time a thing objectively takes, while metterci states the time a person takes. Farcela means to manage, and cavarsela means to get by. The pronouns move with the person: me ne vado, te ne vai.

items.hr: Me ne vado, è tardi e domani lavoro.
items.en: I am off, it is late and I am working tomorrow.
items.note: andarsene: me ne vado, te ne vai, se ne va

items.hr: Non ce la faccio più, sono stanchissima.
items.en: I cannot take any more, I am exhausted.
items.note: farcela: ce la faccio, ce la fai; stanchissima agrees with a female speaker

items.hr: Ci vogliono due ore per arrivare in centro.
items.en: It takes two hours to get to the centre.
items.note: volerci agrees with the time: ci vuole un'ora, ci vogliono due ore

items.hr: Io ci metto venti minuti a piedi.
items.en: It takes me twenty minutes on foot.
items.note: metterci has a personal subject: io ci metto, tu ci metti

items.hr: Con l'italiano me la cavo abbastanza bene.
items.en: I get by quite well in Italian.
items.note: cavarsela: me la cavo, te la cavi, se la cava

activities.title: Quanto ci vuole?
activities.intro: Practise choosing the right pronominal verb and making it agree.

questions.prompt: Which sentence says how long the journey takes in general, not for one person?
questions.explanation: Volerci states the objective time needed. Metterci always has a personal subject.

questions.prompt: Which sentence means I am leaving?
questions.explanation: Andarsene puts the reflexive pronoun first and ne second, giving me ne vado.

questions.prompt: Choose the right form for a plural quantity of time: 'Per andare a Roma ______ tre ore.' (from volerci)
questions.explanation: Volerci agrees with the amount of time, so a plural like tre ore takes ci vogliono.

questions.prompt: Complete with the io form of metterci: 'Da casa al lavoro ______ mezz'ora.'
questions.explanation: Metterci conjugates like mettere with ci in front, so the io form is ci metto.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'I cannot take any more, I am exhausted.'
questions.explanation: Farcela keeps the order ce la before the verb, and non plus più wraps around it.

activities.title: Quanto ci metti ad arrivare?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A friend asks about your commute and you use these verbs naturally.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Quanto ci metti ad arrivare in ufficio la mattina?
lines.en: How long does it take you to get to the office in the morning?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ci metto quaranta minuti, se non c'è traffico.
lines.en: It takes me forty minutes, if there is no traffic.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: E in bicicletta quanto ci vuole?
lines.en: And by bike how long does it take?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ci vogliono venti minuti, ma d'inverno non ce la faccio.
lines.en: It takes twenty minutes, but in winter I cannot manage it.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ti capisco. Con il freddo è dura.
lines.en: I understand you. With the cold it is tough.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Comunque me la cavo, prendo il tram e leggo.
lines.en: Anyway I get by, I take the tram and read.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Bravo. Senti, io me ne vado, ho una riunione.
lines.en: Good for you. Listen, I am off, I have a meeting.

days.title: Il si impersonale
days.objective: Say what people in general do, using si plus the verb, with correct agreement.
days.paretoFocus: Italian has no everyday word for the impersonal one or they, so si carries all of it, from signs and rules to how you ask what something is called.
drills: Describe three habits of your own country using si plus a verb.
Turn three rules of a public place into si può or non si può sentences.
Say five things you eat in a place you know, choosing si mangia or si mangiano correctly.

items: How do you ask what something is called in Italian?
When does the verb after si become plural?
How do you say smoking is not allowed here?

activities.title: In Italia si mangia tardi
activities.intro: Si plus the third person singular means people in general do something, covering English one, you, they and people. The trap is agreement: if a plural object follows, the verb goes plural too, so si mangia il pane but si mangiano le paste. Think of it as the thing being eaten, not the eater.

items.hr: In Italia si mangia tardi la sera.
items.en: In Italy people eat late in the evening.
items.note: no object, so the verb stays singular

items.hr: Come si dice window in italiano?
items.en: How do you say window in Italian?
items.note: the standard way to ask for a word

items.hr: Qui non si può fumare.
items.en: Smoking is not allowed here.
items.note: si può + infinitive for rules and permissions

items.hr: In quel bar si mangiano dei panini ottimi.
items.en: In that bar they serve excellent sandwiches.
items.note: plural object panini, so the verb becomes plural: si mangiano

items.hr: In questa città si vive bene e si spende poco.
items.en: In this city life is good and things are cheap.
items.note: two impersonal verbs sharing one si each; città keeps its accent

activities.title: Come si fa
activities.intro: Practise the impersonal si and the agreement trap that comes with it.

questions.prompt: Choose the correct form: 'In quella trattoria ___ dei dolci buonissimi.'
questions.explanation: Dei dolci is a plural object, so the verb agrees with it and becomes si mangiano.

questions.prompt: Which question asks for the Italian word for something?
questions.explanation: Come si dice asks for the wording. Come si fa asks how something is done.

questions.prompt: Complete with the impersonal pronoun: 'In Italia ______ cena verso le 20:30.'
questions.explanation: Si plus the third person singular expresses what people in general do.

questions.prompt: Make the verb agree with the plural object: 'In quel negozio si ______ molti libri usati.' (from vendere)
questions.explanation: With a plural object the impersonal verb goes into the third person plural, so si vendono.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'Smoking is not allowed here.'
questions.explanation: Non comes before si, and the infinitive follows può unchanged.

activities.title: Come si fa qui?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You ask a local how things are normally done in their town.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Allora, come ti trovi qui?
lines.en: So, how are you finding it here?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Bene, ma non capisco a che ora si mangia la sera.
lines.en: Well, but I do not understand what time people eat in the evening.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Si cena verso le 20:30, prima è difficile trovare posto.
lines.en: People have dinner around 20:30, before that it is hard to find a table.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: E in quel locale all'angolo si mangia bene?
lines.en: And in that place on the corner is the food good?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Sì, si mangiano dei primi ottimi e si spende poco.
lines.en: Yes, they do excellent pasta dishes and it is cheap.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Perfetto. Un'ultima cosa: come si dice this in italiano?
lines.en: Perfect. One last thing: how do you say this in Italian?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Si dice questo. Vedi che stai imparando?
lines.en: You say questo. See that you are learning?

days.title: Il gerundio e stare + gerundio
days.objective: Form the gerund and use stare plus the gerund for actions in progress.
days.paretoFocus: Sto facendo answers the commonest question in any phone call, what are you doing, and the bare gerund compresses two clauses into one.
drills: Say what three people in your home are doing right now.
Turn three how questions into answers using a bare gerund, such as by walking or by reading.
Describe what you were doing at three specific times yesterday using stavo plus a gerund.

items: What are the two gerund endings, and which verbs take each?
Name the three common irregular gerunds.
How do you say what you were doing when something interrupted you?

activities.title: Che cosa stai facendo?
activities.intro: The gerund replaces the infinitive ending with ando for are verbs and endo for ere and ire verbs. With stare it describes an action in progress right now or at a past moment. On its own it means by doing or while doing. Three verbs are irregular: fare gives facendo, bere gives bevendo, dire gives dicendo.

items.hr: Che cosa stai facendo? Sto guardando un film.
items.en: What are you doing? I am watching a film.
items.note: fare gives the irregular gerund facendo

items.hr: Non posso rispondere, sto lavorando.
items.en: I cannot answer, I am working.
items.note: are verbs take ando: lavorare gives lavorando

items.hr: Stavamo mangiando quando è arrivato Luca.
items.en: We were eating when Luca arrived.
items.note: stare in the imperfetto plus the gerund for a past action in progress

items.hr: Ho imparato molto guardando la televisione italiana.
items.en: I learned a lot by watching Italian television.
items.note: bare gerund means by doing, with no preposition

items.hr: Sbagliando si impara, dicono sempre i miei.
items.en: You learn by making mistakes, my parents always say.
items.note: dire gives dicendo; i miei means my parents

activities.title: Sto studiando
activities.intro: Practise forming gerunds and using them with stare.

questions.prompt: Which is the correct gerund of fare?
questions.explanation: Fare builds its gerund on the old stem facere, giving facendo. Fatto is the past participle.

questions.prompt: Which sentence describes an action in progress at a past moment?
questions.explanation: Stare in the imperfetto plus the gerund places the ongoing action in the past.

questions.prompt: Form the gerund: 'In questo momento sto ______ la cena.' (from preparare)
questions.explanation: Are verbs drop are and add ando, giving preparando.

questions.prompt: Form the irregular gerund: 'Sta ______ un caffè al bar.' (from bere)
questions.explanation: Bere comes from the old form bevere, so its gerund is bevendo.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'We were eating when my brother arrived.'
questions.explanation: The ongoing action takes stavo plus the gerund, and the interrupting event takes the passato prossimo.

activities.title: Ti disturbo?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. Someone calls while you are busy and you say what is going on.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ciao! Ti disturbo? Che cosa stai facendo?
lines.en: Hi! Am I disturbing you? What are you doing?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sto preparando la cena, ma dimmi pure.
lines.en: I am making dinner, but go ahead and tell me.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Volevo sapere se stasera esci.
lines.en: I wanted to know whether you are going out this evening.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Non credo, sto aspettando una telefonata importante.
lines.en: I do not think so, I am waiting for an important call.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Capito. Ieri ti ho scritto ma non hai risposto.
lines.en: Got it. Yesterday I messaged you but you did not reply.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Scusa, stavo guidando e poi me ne sono dimenticato.
lines.en: Sorry, I was driving and then I forgot about it.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Figurati. Allora ci sentiamo domani.
lines.en: Do not mention it. So we will talk tomorrow.

days.title: Il trapassato prossimo
days.objective: Use the trapassato prossimo to show that one past event happened before another.
days.paretoFocus: Without it a story flattens into a list of events, and this tense is what lets you say that something had already happened before the point you are describing.
drills: Take three events from yesterday and add, for each, something that had already happened before it.
Say three things you had never done before your last trip.
Retell one short story using all three past tenses, one sentence each.

items: How is the trapassato prossimo built, and which tense is the auxiliary in?
Which word often signals it in a sentence?
Which auxiliary do verbs of movement take, and does the participle agree?

activities.title: Il treno era già partito
activities.intro: The trapassato prossimo is the imperfetto of avere or essere plus the past participle: avevo mangiato, ero partito. It marks the earlier of two past events, so it almost never appears alone. Watch for già and non ancora, which often go with it. With essere the participle still agrees with the subject.

items.hr: Quando sono arrivato in stazione, il treno era già partito.
items.en: When I got to the station, the train had already left.
items.note: the arrival is the later event, the departure the earlier one

items.hr: Non ho comprato il pane perché l'avevo già preso ieri.
items.en: I did not buy bread because I had already got some yesterday.
items.note: the buying is later, the getting earlier

items.hr: Era stanchissima perché aveva lavorato tutto il giorno.
items.en: She was exhausted because she had worked all day.
items.note: imperfetto for the state, trapassato for its earlier cause

items.hr: Ci hanno detto che avevano prenotato una camera doppia.
items.en: They told us that they had booked a double room.
items.note: reported speech pushes the past one step further back

items.hr: Quel film non l'avevo mai visto prima.
items.en: I had never seen that film before.
items.note: with a preceding direct object pronoun the participle agrees: l'avevo visto

activities.title: Prima e dopo
activities.intro: Practise placing one past event before another.

questions.prompt: Choose the right tense: 'Quando siamo usciti, la pioggia ___ già finita.'
questions.explanation: The rain stopped before the going out, so the earlier event takes the imperfetto of essere plus the participle.

questions.prompt: Which sentence says the booking happened before the telling?
options: Ha detto che prenota una camera.
Ha detto che prenoterà una camera.
Ha detto che aveva prenotato una camera.
questions.answer: Ha detto che aveva prenotato una camera.
questions.explanation: Aveva prenotato is the trapassato prossimo and places the booking before the moment of speaking.

questions.prompt: Complete with the imperfetto of avere: 'Non ho mangiato perché ______ già cenato.'
questions.explanation: The trapassato prossimo uses the imperfetto of the auxiliary, so avevo plus the participle.

questions.prompt: Make the participle agree: 'Le mie amiche erano già ______ quando sono arrivato.' (from partire)
questions.explanation: Partire takes essere, so the participle agrees with the feminine plural subject, giving partite.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'She was tired because she had worked all day.'
questions.explanation: The state is in the imperfetto and its earlier cause is in the trapassato prossimo.

activities.title: Sono arrivato tardi
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You explain a missed appointment using all three past tenses.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ieri ti ho aspettato mezz'ora. Che è successo?
lines.en: Yesterday I waited half an hour for you. What happened?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Scusami. Quando sono arrivato in stazione, il treno era già partito.
lines.en: I am sorry. When I got to the station, the train had already left.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: E non hai preso quello dopo?
lines.en: And did you not take the next one?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ci ho provato, ma avevano cancellato la corsa delle 18:10.
lines.en: I tried, but they had cancelled the 18:10 service.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Potevi scrivermi almeno.
lines.en: You could at least have messaged me.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Avevo il telefono scarico, l'avevo lasciato a casa in carica.
lines.en: My phone was dead, I had left it at home charging.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Va bene, capisco. Recuperiamo domani.
lines.en: All right, I understand. We will catch up tomorrow.

days.title: Le congiunzioni perché, siccome, quindi
days.objective: Join two ideas with cause and consequence using perché, siccome, quindi and perciò.
days.paretoFocus: Linking words are what turn short sentences into speech that sounds adult, and these four cover almost all everyday cause and effect.
drills: Take one cause and one result and express the pair three ways, changing the connector each time.
Answer three perché questions with full perché sentences, not single words.
Write two sentences that begin with siccome, each with a comma in the right place.

items: Which connector can start the sentence and which one cannot?
What is the difference between perché as a question and perché as an answer?
Which two words mean so or therefore and introduce the result?

activities.title: Perché, siccome, quindi
activities.intro: Perché introduces the cause and never opens the sentence in a statement. Siccome introduces the cause and always opens the sentence, followed by a comma. Quindi and perciò introduce the result. The same two facts can be arranged with any of them, only the order changes.

items.hr: Non sono uscito perché pioveva.
items.en: I did not go out because it was raining.
items.note: perché + cause, second in the sentence

items.hr: Siccome pioveva, non sono uscito.
items.en: Since it was raining, I did not go out.
items.note: siccome opens the sentence and takes a comma before the main clause

items.hr: Pioveva, quindi non sono uscito.
items.en: It was raining, so I did not go out.
items.note: quindi introduces the consequence

items.hr: Ho perso il treno, perciò sono arrivato in ritardo.
items.en: I missed the train, therefore I arrived late.
items.note: perciò is a slightly stronger quindi and keeps its accent

items.hr: Perché non mi hai chiamato? Perché non avevo campo.
items.en: Why did not you call me? Because I had no signal.
items.note: the same word asks the question and gives the answer

activities.title: Causa e conseguenza
activities.intro: Practise linking a cause to its result with the right connector.

questions.prompt: Which connector must come at the very start of the sentence?
questions.explanation: Siccome always opens the sentence with the cause, followed by a comma and then the result.

questions.prompt: Choose the connector that introduces the result: 'Era tardi, ___ siamo tornati a casa.'
questions.explanation: The second clause is the consequence of the first, so it takes quindi rather than a cause word.

questions.prompt: Complete with the cause connector that cannot open the sentence: 'Sono rimasto a casa ______ stavo male.'
questions.explanation: Perché introduces the reason and stands in the middle of the sentence.

questions.prompt: Complete the opening connector: '______ non avevo la macchina, ho preso l'autobus.' (meaning since)
questions.explanation: Siccome puts the cause first, and the main clause follows after the comma.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'I missed the train, therefore I arrived late.'
questions.explanation: Perciò sits between the two clauses and introduces the consequence.

activities.title: Spiegami il motivo
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You explain a change of plan using all four connectors.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Perché non sei venuto alla riunione?
lines.en: Why did not you come to the meeting?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Perché mio figlio stava male e sono rimasto a casa.
lines.en: Because my son was ill and I stayed at home.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Poteva venire tua moglie a prenderlo.
lines.en: Your wife could have come to pick him up.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Siccome lei era fuori città, ho dovuto occuparmene io.
lines.en: Since she was out of town, I had to deal with it myself.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Capisco. E adesso come sta?
lines.en: I see. And how is he now?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Molto meglio, quindi domani torno in ufficio.
lines.en: Much better, so tomorrow I am back in the office.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Bene, perché domani c'è parecchio da fare.
lines.en: Good, because tomorrow there is quite a lot to do.

days.title: Descrivere una persona, l'aspetto
days.objective: Describe what someone looks like: height, build, hair, eyes and distinguishing features.
days.paretoFocus: Physical description is one of the few topics guaranteed to come up in an oral test, and it drills adjective agreement on every single sentence.
drills: Describe three people you know in three sentences each, working top to bottom.
Describe one person twice, once with essere and once with avere plus the article.
Say who in your family someone resembles, using assomigliare a.

items: Which verb goes with hair and eyes, and which article follows it?
How do you say someone is of average height?
How do you say someone looks younger than they are?

activities.title: Com'è fisicamente
activities.intro: Italian uses essere for overall build and avere plus the definite article for parts of the body: ha i capelli, ha gli occhi, never ha capelli. Hair and eye adjectives are plural because capelli and occhi are plural. Portare covers glasses and beards you choose to wear.

items.hr: Mia sorella è alta e magra, ha i capelli ricci e castani.
items.en: My sister is tall and slim, she has curly chestnut hair.
items.note: avere + i capelli + plural adjectives

items.hr: Porta gli occhiali e ha gli occhi verdi.
items.en: She wears glasses and has green eyes.
items.note: portare gli occhiali, with the article

items.hr: È un ragazzo di media statura, con la barba.
items.en: He is a young man of average height, with a beard.
items.note: di media statura, a fixed phrase

items.hr: Assomiglia molto a suo padre.
items.en: He looks a lot like his father.
items.note: assomigliare a qualcuno; double s and one m

items.hr: Ha circa quarant'anni ma sembra più giovane.
items.en: He is about forty but he looks younger.
items.note: quarant'anni with the apostrophe; sembrare + adjective

activities.title: Chi è quella persona
activities.intro: Practise the verbs, articles and agreements of physical description.

questions.prompt: Which sentence describes someone's hair correctly?
questions.explanation: Italian uses avere plus the definite article with parts of the body, and capelli is plural, so the adjective is plural too.

questions.prompt: How do you say someone is of average height?
questions.explanation: Di media statura is the set phrase. The others are not used in Italian.

questions.prompt: Complete with the verb used for glasses: '______ gli occhiali da sole.' (third person singular, meaning wears)
questions.explanation: Portare covers wearing clothes and accessories, including glasses.

questions.prompt: Complete with the verb meaning to look like: 'Mio figlio ______ molto a sua madre.'
questions.explanation: Assomigliare takes a before the person resembled, and the third person singular is assomiglia.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'My sister is tall and slim and has curly hair.'
questions.explanation: Essere carries the build and avere plus the article carries the hair, with both adjectives agreeing.

activities.title: Come la riconosco?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You describe someone so that your friend can recognise her at the station.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Domani vado a prendere tua cugina, ma non l'ho mai vista.
lines.en: Tomorrow I am picking up your cousin, but I have never seen her.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: È alta, magra, con i capelli ricci e castani.
lines.en: She is tall, slim, with curly chestnut hair.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Quanti anni ha?
lines.en: How old is she?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ha circa trent'anni, ma sembra più giovane.
lines.en: She is about thirty, but she looks younger.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Qualche altro dettaglio?
lines.en: Any other detail?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Porta gli occhiali rotondi e assomiglia molto a mia zia.
lines.en: She wears round glasses and looks a lot like my aunt.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Perfetto, con gli occhiali la riconosco subito.
lines.en: Perfect, with the glasses I will recognise her straight away.

days.title: Descrivere una persona, il carattere
days.objective: Describe someone's personality and say how easy or difficult they are to get on with.
days.paretoFocus: Character adjectives are what you need to talk about colleagues, family and friends, which is most of what people talk about at all.
drills: Choose five character adjectives and apply each to someone you know, agreeing them correctly.
Describe one person with a positive trait and a negative one in the same sentence.
Say who you get on with and who you do not, using andare d'accordo con.

items: Which adjective means nice or likeable, and what is its opposite?
How do you say someone gets on well with everyone?
Which verb phrase means you cannot stand something or someone?

activities.title: Che tipo è?
activities.intro: Simpatico means pleasant company, not sympathetic, and it is the single most used character word in Italian. Andare d'accordo con means to get on with someone, and non sopportare means to be unable to stand something. Every adjective still agrees with the person described.

items.hr: È una persona molto simpatica e disponibile.
items.en: She is a very likeable and helpful person.
items.note: simpatica agrees with persona, which is feminine even for a man

items.hr: Mio fratello è testardo, non cambia mai idea.
items.en: My brother is stubborn, he never changes his mind.
items.note: testardo, with one s and two consonants in the middle

items.hr: È timida con gli sconosciuti, ma con gli amici è allegrissima.
items.en: She is shy with strangers, but with friends she is very cheerful.
items.note: allegrissima, the absolute superlative in issimo

items.hr: Non sopporto la gente maleducata.
items.en: I cannot stand rude people.
items.note: la gente is singular in Italian and takes a singular verb

items.hr: Ha un buon carattere, va d'accordo con tutti.
items.en: He has a good nature, he gets on with everybody.
items.note: andare d'accordo con qualcuno

activities.title: Pregi e difetti
activities.intro: Practise character adjectives and the expressions that go with them.

questions.prompt: What does simpatico describe?
options: someone who feels sorry for others
questions.explanation: Simpatico is about being good company. Feeling sorry for someone is provare compassione, a different idea entirely.

questions.prompt: Which sentence says someone never changes their mind?
questions.explanation: Testardo means stubborn. Timido is shy and allegro is cheerful.

questions.prompt: Make the adjective agree: 'Le mie colleghe sono molto ______.' (from simpatico)
questions.explanation: The feminine plural of simpatico is simpatiche, keeping the hard c sound with an h.

questions.prompt: Complete the expression for getting on with people: 'Va ______ con tutti in ufficio.'
questions.explanation: Andare d'accordo con qualcuno is the fixed phrase for getting on well with someone.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'I cannot stand rude people.'
questions.explanation: La gente is a singular feminine noun, so the adjective maleducata is singular too.

activities.title: Com'è il nuovo collega?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You describe a new colleague's character, good sides and bad.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Allora, com'è il nuovo collega?
lines.en: So, what is the new colleague like?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Molto simpatico e sempre disponibile ad aiutare.
lines.en: Very likeable and always willing to help.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Meno male. L'ultimo era impossibile.
lines.en: Thank goodness. The last one was impossible.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Però è un po' testardo, non cambia mai idea.
lines.en: He is a bit stubborn though, he never changes his mind.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: E con gli altri come si comporta?
lines.en: And how does he behave with the others?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Va d'accordo con tutti, anche con chi è difficile.
lines.en: He gets on with everyone, even with the difficult ones.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Allora è la persona giusta per quel gruppo.
lines.en: Then he is the right person for that team.

days.title: Esprimere i sentimenti
days.objective: Say how you feel and what makes you feel that way, including with mancare and dare fastidio.
days.paretoFocus: Feelings verbs reverse the subject in Italian, so mi manca and mi dà fastidio have to be relearned rather than translated.
drills: Say three things you are afraid of, using avere paura di.
Name two people or places you miss, putting the thing missed as the subject.
Say three things that annoy you, using dare fastidio with the right pronoun.

items: In mi manca la mia famiglia, who or what is the grammatical subject?
Which verb do Italians use with paura, and which preposition follows?
How do you say something bothers you?

activities.title: Come mi sento
activities.intro: Sentirsi plus an adjective says how you feel. The tricky verbs are mancare and dare fastidio, which work like piacere: the thing missed or the thing annoying you is the subject, and you appear as an indirect pronoun. So mi manca la mia famiglia is literally my family is lacking to me.

items.hr: Sono felice di rivederti dopo tanto tempo.
items.en: I am happy to see you again after so long.
items.note: essere felice di + infinitive

items.hr: Oggi mi sento giù, non so perché.
items.en: Today I feel down, I do not know why.
items.note: sentirsi + adjective or adverb

items.hr: Ho paura dei ragni, non li sopporto.
items.en: I am afraid of spiders, I cannot stand them.
items.note: avere paura di + article: di + i = dei

items.hr: Mi dà fastidio il rumore dei lavori.
items.en: The noise of the building work bothers me.
items.note: il rumore is the subject, mi is the person bothered; dà takes an accent

items.hr: Mi manca la mia famiglia, ci sentiamo ogni giorno.
items.en: I miss my family, we talk every day.
items.note: la famiglia is the subject; for a plural you would say mi mancano

activities.title: Mi manca, mi dà fastidio
activities.intro: Practise feelings verbs and the reversed subject they take.

questions.prompt: Which sentence means I miss my friends?
questions.explanation: The people missed are the subject, so a plural subject takes the plural verb mancano.

questions.prompt: Which verb goes with paura in Italian?
questions.explanation: Italian says ho paura, using avere, where English uses to be afraid.

questions.prompt: Complete with the reflexive verb of feeling: 'Oggi non ______ sento bene.'
questions.explanation: Sentirsi is reflexive, so the io form takes mi before the verb.

questions.prompt: Complete with the preposition and article: 'Ho paura ______ cani grossi.' (di plus i)
questions.explanation: Avere paura takes di, and di plus the plural article i contracts to dei.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'The noise bothers me.'
questions.explanation: The thing causing the annoyance is the subject and usually comes after the verb phrase.

activities.title: Ti vedo giù
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A friend notices you are low and you explain how you feel.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ti vedo giù oggi. Va tutto bene?
lines.en: You look down today. Is everything all right?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Non tanto. Mi manca la mia famiglia, è un anno che non la vedo.
lines.en: Not really. I miss my family, it has been a year since I saw them.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Immagino. Non riesci a tornare?
lines.en: I can imagine. Can you not manage to go back?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: A Natale sì, ma ho paura dei voli lunghi.
lines.en: At Christmas yes, but I am afraid of long flights.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ti dà fastidio l'aereo o proprio il viaggio?
lines.en: Is it the plane that bothers you or the journey itself?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: L'aereo. Mi sento male appena decolliamo.
lines.en: The plane. I feel ill as soon as we take off.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Prova a dormire, a me funziona sempre.
lines.en: Try to sleep, it always works for me.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ci provo. Comunque grazie, mi fa piacere parlarne.
lines.en: I will try. Anyway thank you, it is nice to talk about it.

days.title: I numeri ordinali e le date
days.objective: Use ordinal numbers for floors, centuries and sequences, and write dates the Italian way.
days.paretoFocus: Dates and floors come up in every form, appointment and address, and Italian differs from English on exactly the point that matters, the first of the month.
drills: Say the first ten ordinal numbers out loud, then jump to eleventh, twentieth and twenty-first.
Write five dates that matter to you in the Italian format.
Say which floor five different places are on, using al plus the ordinal.

items: Which is the only day of the month that uses an ordinal number?
How do ordinals above ten get built from cardinal numbers?
How do you say a full date with the weekday in front?

activities.title: Il primo, il secondo, il ventunesimo
activities.intro: The first ten ordinals are irregular and must be learned: primo, secondo, terzo, quarto, quinto, sesto, settimo, ottavo, nono, decimo. From eleven on you drop the last vowel of the cardinal and add esimo. Dates take cardinal numbers except the first of the month, which is il primo.

items.hr: Abito al terzo piano, senza ascensore.
items.en: I live on the third floor, with no lift.
items.note: a + il = al before the ordinal

items.hr: Il primo maggio è festa in tutta Italia.
items.en: The first of May is a public holiday all over Italy.
items.note: the only day of the month with an ordinal

items.hr: Sono nato il 3 maggio 1990.
items.en: I was born on 3 May 1990.
items.note: il + figure + month, no preposition before the month

items.hr: Oggi è lunedì 5 marzo.
items.en: Today is Monday 5 March.
items.note: lunedì carries the accent, and the weekday takes no article here

items.hr: È la seconda volta che vengo in questa città.
items.en: It is the second time I have come to this city.
items.note: ordinals agree in gender and number: seconda volta

activities.title: Che giorno è oggi
activities.intro: Practise ordinals, floors and Italian date format.

questions.prompt: Which date is written correctly in Italian?
questions.explanation: Dates use cardinal numbers with no di before the month. Only the first of the month uses the ordinal primo.

questions.prompt: Which is the correct ordinal for twenty-one?
questions.explanation: You drop the final vowel of ventuno and add esimo, giving ventunesimo.

questions.prompt: Complete with the ordinal for the opening day of the month: 'Il ______ gennaio è Capodanno.'
questions.explanation: The first day of the month is the one exception and takes the ordinal primo.

questions.prompt: Complete with the contracted preposition used for floors: 'L'ufficio è ______ quarto piano.'
questions.explanation: Floors take a plus il, which contracts to al before the ordinal.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'It is the second time I have come here.'
questions.explanation: The ordinal agrees with volta, and Italian keeps the present tense after che in this structure.

activities.title: Un appuntamento il 3 maggio
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You fix an appointment and confirm the date, the time and the floor.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Buongiorno, per l'appuntamento va bene lunedì 5 marzo?
lines.en: Good morning, is Monday 5 March all right for the appointment?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Quel giorno lavoro. Possiamo fare martedì 6?
lines.en: I am working that day. Can we do Tuesday the 6th?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Martedì 6 alle 14:35, va bene?
lines.en: Tuesday the 6th at 14:35, is that all right?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Perfetto. E a che piano siete?
lines.en: Perfect. And what floor are you on?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Al quarto piano, l'ascensore è a sinistra dell'entrata.
lines.en: On the fourth floor, the lift is to the left of the entrance.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Grazie. È la seconda volta che vengo, ma non ricordavo.
lines.en: Thank you. It is the second time I have come, but I did not remember.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Nessun problema. Ci vediamo martedì, allora.
lines.en: No problem. See you on Tuesday, then.

days.title: Ripassare e raccontare, la prova A2
days.objective: Tell one connected story that uses all three past tenses, object pronouns and the functional language of the last lessons.
days.paretoFocus: Exam tasks and real conversations both reward one long connected turn far more than a series of correct isolated sentences.
drills: Tell one real journey in eight sentences, using at least one trapassato prossimo and two pronouns.
Inside the same story, include one complaint, one refusal and one suggestion.
Retell it a second time out loud, timing yourself, and aim to speak without stopping.

items: Which tense carries the events, which the background, and which the earlier events?
Which four connectors link cause and result in a story?
Which structure lets you say what people generally do in a place?

activities.title: Una storia intera
activities.intro: Nothing new here: this is one short story that uses everything from the last lessons. Read each line and name the structure it uses, then rebuild the whole story from the English alone. The point is to move between structures without stopping.

items.hr: L'anno scorso siamo andati in Sardegna, ma il viaggio è cominciato male.
items.en: Last year we went to Sardinia, but the trip started badly.
items.note: passato prossimo for the events of the story

items.hr: Quando siamo arrivati in stazione, il treno era già partito e faceva un caldo insopportabile.
items.en: When we got to the station the train had already left and it was unbearably hot.
items.note: trapassato for the earlier event, imperfetto for the background

items.hr: Siccome non c'era un altro treno, abbiamo dovuto aspettare tre ore.
items.en: Since there was no other train, we had to wait three hours.
items.note: siccome opens with the cause

items.hr: Mi sono lamentato alla biglietteria e me lo hanno cambiato senza problemi.
items.en: I complained at the ticket office and they changed it for me without any trouble.
items.note: me lo, the combined pronoun for the ticket

items.hr: Alla fine è stata una bella vacanza: lì si mangia benissimo e si spende poco.
items.en: In the end it was a lovely holiday: the food there is excellent and it is cheap.
items.note: closing judgement plus two impersonal si verbs

activities.title: Ripasso generale
activities.intro: Practise pulling the tenses, pronouns and connectors of this block together.

questions.prompt: Choose the tense that marks the earlier of two past events: 'Quando siamo usciti, loro ___ già mangiato.'
questions.explanation: The eating happened before the going out, so it takes the imperfetto of avere plus the participle.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses the impersonal si with correct agreement?
options: In quel bar si beve dei caffè ottimi.
In quel bar si bevono dei caffè ottimi.
In quel bar si bevo dei caffè ottimi.
In quel bar si è bevuto dei caffè ottimi.
questions.answer: In quel bar si bevono dei caffè ottimi.
questions.explanation: The plural object dei caffè pulls the impersonal verb into the plural, giving si bevono.

questions.prompt: Complete with the combined pronoun meaning it to me: 'Ho chiesto il rimborso e ______ hanno dato subito.'
questions.explanation: Mi becomes me before another pronoun, and the two are written separately before the verb: me lo hanno dato.

questions.prompt: Complete with the imperfetto that describes the weather at the time: 'Quel giorno ______ un caldo terribile.' (from fare)
questions.explanation: Weather as a background condition takes the imperfetto, and the third person singular of fare is faceva.

questions.prompt: Build the Italian for: 'In the end it was a lovely holiday.'
questions.explanation: Essere in the passato prossimo agrees with the feminine subject vacanza, giving è stata.

activities.title: Raccontami tutto
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. Tell the whole story in one connected turn, mixing tenses, pronouns and connectors.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Allora, raccontami com'è andato il viaggio in Sardegna.
lines.en: So, tell me how the trip to Sardinia went.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: È cominciato male: quando siamo arrivati in stazione, il treno era già partito.
lines.en: It started badly: when we got to the station, the train had already left.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Che rabbia. E come avete fatto?
lines.en: How annoying. And what did you do?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Siccome non c'era una coincidenza prima di sera, mi sono lamentato alla biglietteria.
lines.en: Since there was no connection before the evening, I complained at the ticket office.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: E ti hanno cambiato il biglietto?
lines.en: And did they change your ticket?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sì, me lo hanno cambiato subito, quindi abbiamo perso solo mezza giornata.
lines.en: Yes, they changed it for me right away, so we only lost half a day.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Meno male. E poi vi siete trovati bene?
lines.en: Just as well. And then did you enjoy it?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Benissimo. Lì si mangia molto bene e si spende poco: ci torniamo l'anno prossimo.
lines.en: Really well. The food there is very good and it is cheap: we are going back next year.
